### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-05-08T22:03:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-questions-on-changes-from-bulletproof-to-epi-paleo-and-saving-money.6996/post-85664>

Hey all! I had some quick questions on some dietary changes I've been thinking about making. I have been thoroughly studying the bulletproof diet for the past two months, literally listening to and annotating and then summarizing every podcast as well as every appearance Dave makes on another podcast. I started doing the same to his Better Baby Book as well. I've then come across this blog/forum and have been swept away. I agree with Dave on some things, and then I agree with Jacks/the posters here opinions. To cut to the chase, I'm on an **extremely** **limited budget**. I pay monthly to do **Brazilian Jiu Jitsu** and I really love doing that and want to compete at a high level, so that takes some of my money away. My **younger brother is extremely ill** and that has taken a toll on finances drastically and my family lives paycheck to paycheck (as we have for the past 2-3yrs). I have been using what's left of my paycheck money to eat a bulletproof/paleo/epi-paleo template. My questions are:

1) I can get wild caught pink salmon from Whole Foods for $2.99 per 14.75oz, but it is in a can. This is much cheaper than purchasing grass-fed beef from Trader Joe's for $6-10lb, and much more nutritious (I would imagine) than eating tons of non-pasture raised/organic/grass-fed beef, chicken, pork,etc. I can also purchase wild caught clams (in a can) at Trader Joe's for about the same price. Should I make this change so I can consume more shellfish/fatty fish as it is MUCH cheaper, or should I just eat a normal Paleo template until I have money after I get a raise/new job (I'm talking not all grass-fed, incorporating chicken, etc.)? I'm worried about BPA and other xenoestrogens, but I feel as though the nutritional power of the fish/shellfish can maybe makeup for it. Would buying farmed fish be okay for saving money, or since it's basically the equivalent (or worse) of corn-fed/soy-fed antiobiotic filled beef, should I completely stay away?

2) I've stopped buying organic eggs and veggies, does this matter? Should I just stop buying eggs altogether and focus on fish/shellfish, or should I continue to eat eggs as I can normally get 18-24 for about $2 (not organic nor pasture raised). Would it matter if I consume these eggs raw? I know you can clean the eggs with a solution made up of iodine and one other compound to kill/limit salmonella, maybe I should do that and start eating a lot of raw eggs as well?

3) I've been thinking about just consuming a regular paleo template until I have more money, but then just saving my money for what have been the three biggest performance enhancers/symptom eradicators (for me) I have come across - Upgraded Coffee, Upgraded MCT Oil + any grass-fed butter, and magnesium. I was thinking about saving money on food and consuming a normal paleo template, but then just making sure EVERYDAY I do a few things: consume 1-2c of Upgraded Coffee, consume 1-2tbsp of Upgraded MCT Oil for ketogenesis and mental focus, consume 1-2c of anti-angiogenesis tea, and consume literally an entire stick of grass-fed butter a day (about 8tbsp). I would also be taking ice cold showers 10mins a day, with occasional cold baths and I also ground for about 20-45mins a day doing Foundation Training in a field

4) Should I focus on making these "detox" purchases - a showerhead filter, buying this athletic organic shampoo/bodywash/facewash/foot soak recommended on Marks Daily Apple, making my own toothpaste without fluoride, and purchasing about 10-15gallons of RO water from Whole Foods to drink a week?

Throughout the changes I've experienced in mental/psychological symptoms, along with the increased physical performance, I can't shy away from Bulletproof diet/Epi-Paleo, but I simply have such little funds these days. I'm trying to stay as "bulletproof/optimal" as possible. I'm hoping that I can just start doing some DEEP Cold Thermogenesis soon on a daily basis along with daily grounding to at least eradicate some of the negatives of not eating a 100% bulletproof diet; also I will focus on cooking toxins presented in the Better Baby Book (Maillard Reaction and denatured proteins). If anyone could give me their input, I'd be more than gracious, I won't even be able to explain.

### Shijin13 — 2013-05-09T01:56:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-questions-on-changes-from-bulletproof-to-epi-paleo-and-saving-money.6996/post-85675>

Here's my 2cents. eat as much seafood and shellfish you can afford, while canned or farmed may not be the best seafood, its still better than grassfed beef/pork nutrition wise on any day. If you can find an international market and see about getting mackerel - which is an inexpensive fatty fish like salmon but its lower on the food chain. at our local international market I can get 1Lb of frozen mixed seafood for $3.99/lb - that same 1lb bag of frozen mixed seafood at trader joes is $11.99/lb. I realize not every one has access to international markets its is worth trying to find one b/c they're so much cheaper that traditional stores

as for everything else, coffee is my one vise. If I'm going to drink it I want to drink BP - b/c if I don't want to drink it the next day then at least I won't end up with a migraine.

Costco sells Kerry Gold for 2.28/brick. you can also get good seafood from there as well. Do you have access to a yard to plant a garden? what about container gardening if that's not an option. We garden - it does save us on our veggie bill year round, especially with the collards, kale and other greens.

apply the things you can, and don't worry about the things you can't. not everyone can throw the "Whole Kitchen Sink" at their health, those that can't do need to understand that it will take longer this way. but as long as you're good with it and the progress you make on your time frame - then I see your plan as your road to success!

### nonchalant — 2013-05-09T04:14:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-questions-on-changes-from-bulletproof-to-epi-paleo-and-saving-money.6996/post-85693>

Welcome, Papa!

1\. I agree with Shijin13. Any seafood is good seafood. Sardines and clams and small, frozen shrimp and such are pretty cheap. I have stopped buying grass-fed, to save money for seafood. I do buy grass-fed pork fat, however, to render for lard.
2\. I found a local supplier of eggs on Craigslist. I pay $2.50/doz, but I would eat them raw without hesitation.
3\. Refined Coconut oil is fairly inexpensive, as an MCT oil.
4\. I use a very small amount of soap, mostly for cleaning my feet (grounding is dirty work). You can make your own shampoo with baking power & water, and for a conditioner, cucumber and lemon; you may like that. You will find that when you cut the carbs, you cut the body odor. I definitely recommend making your own toothpaste (or powder.) I have been using a baking soda/salt combination with added ground clove for flavor, and I love it. Making your own body cleaning products will save you lots of money. If you can get the water for about $0.40 per gallon, that would be a good idea.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-05-09T09:18:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-questions-on-changes-from-bulletproof-to-epi-paleo-and-saving-money.6996/post-85713>

Thanks for everyone's input thus far!! So I suppose eating canned seafood is not the worst offender to my health?

For those who asked, I'am both trying to reclaim my health, and performance driven. As far as I know (from checkups) I don't suffer from adrenal fatigue/diabetes/etc. and from the mirror test + the fact that I can fast for a whole day with nothing easily, I'm not LR. Dr. Kruse has actually commented on one of my PaleoHacks posts: [http://paleohacks.com/questions/175...he-topic-of-my-bizarre-symptoms#axzz2ROLhaPzh](http://paleohacks.com/questions/175553/last-question-on-the-topic-of-my-bizarre-symptoms#axzz2ROLhaPzh). I essentially suffer from some psychological (possibly stemming from biological roots) symptoms and sensations from ingesting PCP or embalming fluid (this was not a purposeful choice, believe me). I know these substances are anticholingeric, and pairing that with the fact that I suffered during the trip, well I didn't have a bad one but it was worrisome and I lost sleep that whole weekend. For months I was having extreme anxiety, migraines + aura's, and visual disturbances such as objects I look at shimmering or slightly moving/vibrating. It's been 15 months almost and going paleo has been the greatest aid, followed by incorporating Jack/Dave Asprey's protocols. I'am also performance driven as I'm an ex-wrestler and football player, and started up Jiu-Jitsu again and I dream to compete.... and I mean compete at a high level (national/international). Some may call me insane for thinking that I can, but then again people think I'm insane for wearing orange glasses at night and I feel that is more than normal, lol.

I will go to the local Indian and Mexican Markets near my house, I don't have an Asian one, but I'm suspecting I can at least purchase bulk herbs/spices from there and maybe a lot of coconut oil/milk/cream. So is the canned clams/salmon a better choice than the Trader Joe's New Zealand Grass-fed Ground Beef? What about the eggs - should I discontinue them as I know they are o6 skewed? But they do have many valuable nutrients. How about the water, out of all the things after skimming through the latest blogs (I'm actually reading every blog in the Leptin Prescription and annotating them and then going to read/annotate all the citations that were given, then read/annotate/review Mastering Leptin so I can fully understand everything there is to know about Leptin) I think water may be one of the more important variables - both drinking and bathing water.

So here is what I'm thinking thus far until I have the money to do more:
(1) Set aside money to purchase weekly RO water in separate 5 gallon jugs from Whole Foods - buy about 3 of them which can be refilled for roughly $2 each; try and drink roughly a gallon a day
(2) To save money (for now) eat all the canned seafood I can an any un-canned seafood I can find for reasonable prices; drop the grass-fed beef; for variety/other nutrients eat non-grass-fed meat but make sure they're primarily lean cuts eaten rare and I do everything possible to limit cooking toxins; Drink 1-2C of Upgraded Coffee per day but decaf after 2PM; rather than spending tons of money on coconut oil which has less MCT's (although other beneficial FA) consume 1-2tbsp of Upgraded MCT Oil per day; Consume much more salt everyday (trader joe's sea salt & Onnit brand Himalayan Salt); Consume a stick of butter a day for vit.A and butyric acid
(3) As I don't believe in some supplements (Vit.D included), continue supplementing Magnesium, but switch to a more sublingual route or dump magnesium into baths rather than taking my chelated magnesium glycinate
(4) DEEP CT... even though it is summer. I'm talking about 10min ice cold showers everyday, with weekly ice cold baths, drinking ice cold water before and after, and keeping all my rooms as cool as possible with minimal clothing... eventually getting a Cool Fat Burner to wear while on my laptop
(5) Grounding+sunlight as much as possible

Any comments on what I'm thinking up so far? For added info, I will soon be getting a check up through WellnessFX as I earned a free check up, and I'm soon going to purchase a blood glucose/blood ketone meter to do some bio-hacks/experiments on myself, I will post results when I can. Btw, I will be doing Bulletproof Intermittent Fasting most days but I normally go longer than 12hrs without the BPC unlike some BP-IF practitioners. Should I focus on emf/artificial light? I already have blue-light blocking glasses + f.lux and a dimmed screen, but I was thinking of purchasing those greenwave emf filters for my bedroom and getting all the electronics besides my laptop out of the room.

Thank you so much everyone, believe me I appreciate it more than you would expect. I hope to some day be doing what Jack is doing for people as he has been an aid to recovery to so many. Thanks again.

### caroline — 2013-05-09T10:56:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-questions-on-changes-from-bulletproof-to-epi-paleo-and-saving-money.6996/post-85717>

You are doing great and are really giving all this a lot of thought.
Have you thought of eating lamb heart and liver? Some of us eat lamb heart raw - carpaccio style..... and as I said above you can make a pate from lamb liver very quickly and cheaply. The MHS \[mackerel head smoothy\] is very cheap and great brain food. Some us also eat raw beef - I just stick a piece of rump on a wooden board and leave in the fridge. After about a week it has started to dry and you eat far less than cooked - at least that is my experience. That is what I have for breakfast along with 2 BPC.

I am curious about the BPC - you seem to feel very strongly about keeping that in your diet - may I ask why? and you seem to feel you get much more benefit from MCT oil?

Make sure that you go over Jack'c recommended list of labs ......Vitamin D3 is very important - maybe read more on that and absolutely for sure get Vit D tested. I think the blog is called - the sunshine of your life.

### Dali Dula — 2013-05-09T12:36:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-questions-on-changes-from-bulletproof-to-epi-paleo-and-saving-money.6996/post-85719>

Bone broth from grass fed beef and fish broth from fish heads/ bodies/crustacean shells. These can be made inexpensive and are nutritious. Get an electric crock pot and throw bones and stuff in with some kombu, cook for many hours. Eat this straight up or use it as a base for other things. Powerful ,cheap, easy.

I'm also coming at this from the performance perspective as I'm an ex-wrestler and football player, and I continue to do BJJ and I want to compete at the highest level (national/international). I would do MMA competitively, but I have suffered 2 serious concussions and cracked my head open before, and I'm not trying to take precious years off my life... especially because I'm engaged as of today!

I take it that I should focus on fish consumption regardless of quality over grass-fed beef? Even canned/farmed would be better than buying Grass-fed beef right now? I'm just worried about irradiated and polluted sources, I could care less about the heavy metals because I only eat small amounts of tuna and I normally drink tea with the fish which has been shown to bind to mercury, and I drink 5-6glasses a day anyhow as of now.

Should I be focusing on the fish, and not worry if it is farmed or canned right now? So far this week I consumed a couple of grain-fed ribeye steaks but cut off the extra fat so I didn't consume as much o6, and I DRENCHED it in kerrygold butter. For those who asked, I'm adamant on the BPC as it does give me a noticeable performance boost, and it is a superfood (I'm convinced). Eventually when the funds allow, I will get an AeroLatte and a metal Aeropress filter to better emulsify the fats and to allow the anti-inflammatory oils to stay intact (kawhol, cafestol... sorry about the spelling).

I can't even express how much I appreciate everyone's input thus far! It's greatly appreciated. I hope to be doing what Dr.Kruse does one day, working in the medical field is now a desire/dream of mine, and I've learned so much through him, Dave, and the members on both forums. I have been inspired to take up a useful genetics course through this site: [https://www.coursera.org/;](https://www.coursera.org/;) I recommend anyone seeking additional education to check this site out, all the courses are free through prestigious universities, and you can get the grades/transcripts uploaded to an account so you can then use it on a resume or application to college/jobs/etc. Thanks again!

### caroline — 2013-05-15T10:13:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-questions-on-changes-from-bulletproof-to-epi-paleo-and-saving-money.6996/post-86071>

Congratulations on your engagement! How wonderful ....I hope all your dreams come true. You are on the journey of a lifetime and you definitely are up for the challenge. I can see that your are totally committed and 100% all in - how amazing for all of us to watch you grow and blossom and take charge and make your future what you want it to be......

Yes - fish is better than grassfed beef .....but don't cut the fat off the grassfed steaks!

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-05-16T18:30:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-questions-on-changes-from-bulletproof-to-epi-paleo-and-saving-money.6996/post-86218>

Thank you! Believe me, people on these boards will see some things, although it may take some time. I plan on running some cool bio-hacks/experiments on myself and quantifying (similar to Barry's ketosis+athletics, except I'll quantify it with my blood glucose and blood ketone readings as well as perceived fatigue, energy, etc.). I know, I never cut the fat off grass-fed meat, I was speaking about grain-fed steaks; they were fatty so I didn't consume most of the fat, and I only lightly cooked it to reduce HCA's, Maillard Reaction, etc.

So am I getting this right..... the poorest sources of fish should still be sought after, even over better sources of meat? If so I have no problems consuming a few pounds of canned salmon a week and then just try and balance it out with some clams or mussels, once and awhile some red meat.

I asked the same question on the Bulletproof Forums and I was told to refrain from eating canned/farmed fish at all costs and save my money almost exclusively for grass-fed beef+grass-fed butter. Well, after only skimming through a couple Brain-Gut posts (as I don't like delving into it unless I can take notes and annotate everything), as well as a couple forum posts, I think that is not as much of a worry. I know there is irradiated water, polluted water (oil spills), gmo fish, etc. but grass-fed meat is completely out of the question for me right now outside of the occasional pound or two a week or less (biweekly), grain-fed meat, non-organic meat is probably worse than farmed fish because at least fish have an overwhelming amount of o3 (especially DHA) and astaxanthan and other compounds that at least diminish the other aspects of it.

Thanks everyone who has responded thus far!

By the way, I really appreciate how you come on these boards and engage everyone, whether giving out inspiring words, summarizing massive amounts of information for us, giving recommendations, or openly taking on people who wish to debate... it's great and most other paleo/primal/health boards don't get that kind of action from the person who started it all. I'm going to take your advice and for now eat as much seafood as possible, even canned/farmed if I must. # Thread: Sleep, ya big dummy! No sleep = propensity for developing Cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ya-big-dummy-no-sleep-propensity-for-developing-cancer.7007/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dextery — 2013-05-09T21:07:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ya-big-dummy-no-sleep-propensity-for-developing-cancer.7007/post-85745>

[http://www.lef.org/news/LefDailyNew...twitter&utm\_medium=social&utm\_campaign=normal](http://www.lef.org/news/LefDailyNews.htm?NewsID=19348&Section=Disease&utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=normal)

"Melatonin, the hormone produced by the pineal gland and responsible for regulating our sleep-wake cycles, is widely thought to be at the heart of this link between sleep and cancer. In the lab, researchers have found that higher melatonin levels suppress tumor growth; levels close to those found in people with too much exposure to artificial light seem to promote more aggressive tumor growth in cancer cells."

Dr Kruse in [http://www.jackkruse.com/why-do-we-sleep/](http://www.jackkruse.com/why-do-we-sleep/)
tells us the importance of sleep and cancer.

> It appears that sleep directly effects the chronic diseases of aging and likely plays a role in cancer development.

Follow this link to the blog index and scroll down to the category "sleep" for the other blog post pearls of wisdom regarding sleep.
[http://www.jackkruse.com/blog-index/](http://www.jackkruse.com/blog-index/)

Anybody still think sleep is not important to optimal health?

# Thread: Little buzzing buggers - conquered?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/little-buzzing-buggers-conquered.7008/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2013-05-09T22:06:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/little-buzzing-buggers-conquered.7008/post-85752>

Yes, mosquitos! Had the most curious thing happen on the weekend. When there are mosquitos, I usually feel them biting me before I even notice they're out. Yes, I'm the one that's always covered in bites - after camping have counted 100 bites on my legs alone.

We were outside Sunday evening, and I could hear them buzzing and actually caught a couple. And I didn't get one bit. Huh?? Felt a bit weird, but so exciting! Or maybe I'm just too sour for the pesky little buggers. LOL. Am wondering what's made a difference - so I can keep it up! I hate bug spray - but I'm sure someone here will have a great natural repellent idea!

Or maybe I won't need it.....

### agatha — 2013-05-10T17:00:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/little-buzzing-buggers-conquered.7008/post-85789>

That's very convenient if they little blighters don't like you any more. I have heard others say this too after going low carb.

### cantweight — 2013-05-10T18:29:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/little-buzzing-buggers-conquered.7008/post-85791>

I always thought it had something to do with sweat or how much CO2 your body puts off....my daughter and i rarely get bit while my husband and sons get eaten up. Would love to know the answer..glad those suckers are leaving you be!!!

### Dextery — 2013-05-10T23:24:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/little-buzzing-buggers-conquered.7008/post-85808>

> [diane said:](/goto/post?id=85752)
>
> Yes, mosquitos! Had the most curious thing happen on the weekend. When there are mosquitos, I usually feel them biting me before I even notice they're out. Yes, I'm the one that's always covered in bites - after camping have counted 100 bites on my legs alone.
>
> We were outside Sunday evening, and I could hear them buzzing and actually caught a couple. And I didn't get one bit. Huh?? Felt a bit weird, but so exciting! Or maybe I'm just too sour for the pesky little buggers. LOL. Am wondering what's made a difference - so I can keep it up! I hate bug spray - but I'm sure someone here will have a great natural repellent idea!
>
> Or maybe I won't need it.....
>

I suspect the reason is the consumption of copious amounts of coherent spring water. But I certainly don't know why the critters don't like your blood now. Maybe it is your now smooth skin.

I recall that mosquitos are attracted by exhalation of carbon dioxide as Cantweight said and maybe the water has cut down on CO2 exhalation.

### GlenPDQ — 2013-05-11T08:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/little-buzzing-buggers-conquered.7008/post-85830>

Ketones repel mosquitos!

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/...t-smelling-sweat-keeps-mosquitoes-at-bay.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/science-news/6142961/Sweet-smelling-sweat-keeps-mosquitoes-at-bay.html)
Sweet smelling sweat keeps mosquitoes at bay - Telegraph
[www.telegraph.co.uk](http://www.telegraph.co.uk) › Science › Science News‎
by Richard Gray - in 64 Google+ circles
Sep 5, 2009 – Now scientists have discovered key differences in the body odours ... The researchers found that these compounds, known as ketones, repel mosquitoes. ... New repellent could stop mosquitoes by confusing their smell ...

For what its worth, the other day a female coworker told me "you smell good!"
I don't wear cologne or a fragrance at work, so maybe she was detecting some of those fruity ketones. I had some fun with her and said its the cologne I use called Midnight Pasture.

### SeaHorse — 2013-05-11T15:26:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/little-buzzing-buggers-conquered.7008/post-85837>

Another good reason to stay ketosis during the summer! On a related note....I found that the first couple of bug bites of the season did not send me into a huge over reaction...a little redness, a little itching and then they both faded.This is a HUGE difference for me as I was often driven into a giant state of craziness by my bug bite reactions plus I, like you Diane, was the original bug magnet in my family. For the last few years I've been wearing an overshirt and hat that have the bug repellant impregnated in the fabric...not ideal but it made me get through the summers without insanity.

### BJK77 — 2013-05-10T15:42:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-estrogen.7013/post-85785>

My estrogen levels are low - labs show it and symptoms are consistent with it: hot flashes, dry, no libido, etc. I finally found a doc who will prescribe me a large enough dose of Progesterone to get those levels optimal, but he will not prescribe estrogen because of my age. He told me yesterday my labs look like those of a 50-year old woman and if I were 50 he would prescribe the estrogen, but he can't "in good conscience" prescribe it for me at my age. That makes no sense to me at all, but the many other docs I went to would never give me enough Pg, so I feel like I need to stick with him and take the estrogen matters into my own hands until I can find someone better.

I found I could order Estrogen from an online pharmacy, but they are all european variations and all my google searches aren't showing me ingredients or anything about these forms. Is anyone familiar with any of them: [http://goldpharma.com/search/estradiol/lang/ENGLISH/](http://goldpharma.com/search/estradiol/lang/ENGLISH/) I don't like that it says "Natural and semisynthetic" at the top. I want to know I'm getting completely natural stuff!

I also found this option: [http://www.1-menopause.com/order.htm](http://www.1-menopause.com/order.htm) Is anyone familiar with this product?

### BJK77 — 2013-05-13T17:34:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-estrogen.7013/post-85945>

Thanks so much for all the suggestions.

Nuttmegs - Do you workk with Dzugan? I've been reading/hearing a lot of complaints about that program lately, so given the cost I've been a bit hesitant to go that route.

Gretchen - Do you still work with Dzugan? Have you been pretty pleased with your experience? You find him to listen well and work with you instead of telling you what to do?

Darleen - Thanks for the link to the Wiley site. I loved that book and had no idea there was a list of providers who use the Wiley protocol. I found one about 1-1.5 hours from me . . . not too far. I'm going to call this morning to see if I can get an appt. The only thing that worries me is the website talks about this doctor using a 'membership/concierge' approach to medicine and says she only enrolls 150-200 patients. \*fingers crossed\* she still has openings and it's not ridiculously expensive!

### nuttmegs17 — 2013-05-13T18:30:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-estrogen.7013/post-85948>

> [BJK77 said:](/goto/post?id=85945)

>

>
> Gretchen - Do you still work with Dzugan? Have you been pretty pleased with your experience? You find him to listen well and work with you instead of telling you what to do?
>
> Darleen - Thanks for the link to the Wiley site. I loved that book and had no idea there was a list of providers who use the Wiley protocol. I found one about 1-1.5 hours from me . . . not too far. I'm going to call this morning to see if I can get an appt. The only thing that worries me is the website talks about this doctor using a 'membership/concierge' approach to medicine and says she only enrolls 150-200 patients. \*fingers crossed\* she still has openings and it's not ridiculously expensive!

Hello! Yes, I work with Dzugan. Overall I like it. I have had a few problems but they are not Dzugan specific....for whatever reason, my body really fights hormones supps. This was even before BHRT. For instance, I have hashi's, and it took me MONTHS to get to a theraputic dose of thyroid meds bc I just had bad side affects. Therefore my motto is LOW and SLOW. Like whatever they normally prescribe, I usually take at least a 1/4 of what they suggest. I think Dzugan knows his stuff and I like that Gretchen and Colleen use him and talk to them bc I think they are getting some education from Jack along with what they know works for instance. My biggest complaint - but this would be true of all of these type of BHRT programs - is that it takes several days to get an update. For instance, I put in an update last week and am still waiting for them to get back to me. It can take 3-5 days. If you happen to be having a bad side effect that is a long time to wait. However, during those times, I usually come here and ask questions and people like Colleen, Gretchen, etc are really helpful until I hear back from them.

They also seem to listen to suggestion about what forms you want to use. FOr instance I am not hearing great things about cream supplementation so am going to inquire about getting sublingual. I have no doubt they will accommadate that.

I hope this helps!

Yes I'm using Dzugan - via his [www.dzlogic.com](http://www.dzlogic.com) program. I'm very satisfied, though I really had to advocate for what I wanted. I've been migraine free for two months - up until the stress of last week caught up w/me on Friday. but a little extra hormones helped me deal with and I was able to function, unlike previously.

### Lanie — 2013-05-14T01:55:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-estrogen.7013/post-85975>

> [DarleenMB said:](/goto/post?id=85795)
>
> wileyprotocol.com
>
> find a local provider.

I think Darleen and I have agreed to disagree about Wiley. Just be aware that it's not right for everyone. I was on the Wiley Protocol for 7 or 8 months. It's relatively inexpensive (i.e., there's no $1900 sign-up fee, like with Dzugan). The hormones cost about $100-$150 a month, in addition to blood draws and doc appointments.

Some things to be aware of: Wiley practitioners, if doing the protocol correctly, do blood draws to measure hormone levels rather than saliva. Measuring via saliva is often considered a more accurate measurement, as it quantifies the amount of hormone in the system that is free and available for the body to use. Blood tests may present a different picture but not clearly show how the body is using the hormones. See here for more info:

[http://www.drmarcantel.com/hormone-testing-saliva-or-serum-blood-test/](http://www.drmarcantel.com/hormone-testing-saliva-or-serum-blood-test/)

An additional issue I had was with the huge doses of hormones prescribed via the Wiley Protocol. After 6 or 7 months I became very bloated, I gained weight, my breasts increased 1-2 cup sizes (they're already too big), I was tired all the time, and had heart palpitations. My belly started protruding - it looked like I was 6 months pregnant. I continued to have vicious pre-menstrual mood swings. My periods got much weirder. My cycles would last 30-40 days total, and periods would last 10 days, including 1-2 days of obnoxiously heavy flow. My doc (who was also on Wiley) told me that sometimes she didn't have a period for months (she's around 40).

All of these things (except the weight gain, unfortunately) resolved within a month of discontinuing the hormones. Now I'm back to a 27-28 day cycle, my belly isn't sticking out so far anymore (even though I haven't lost the weight), and my bras fit again. Please google "Progesterone toxicity symptoms." I had a lot of the symptoms. While on the protocol my labs indicated my progesterone levels were mid-range on Day 12 (0.6 with range 0.2-1.5) and the low end of the desired range for Day 21 (3.0 with range 1.7-27.0).

TS Wiley is not a doctor - she has a BA in Anthropology, I think. Apparently some people feel great on this protocol, but just be aware that others have had problems.

I hope that helps.

### BJK77 — 2013-05-14T21:36:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-estrogen.7013/post-86036>

Thanks for sharing this Lanie. Definitely gives me something to think about . . . it just seems as if there's really no good answer! I've always been told saliva testing was more accurate for hormones as well and didn't realize the Wiley protocol called for blood testing instead. What you've typed below makes me assume the Wiley protocol isn't big on listening to patients and is more about "do this because it's what Wiley says." That's not at all what I'm looking for.

Gretchen or Nuttmegs - Do you do salivary or blood testing with Dzugan?

> [Lanie said:](/goto/post?id=85975)
>
> I think Darleen and I have agreed to disagree about Wiley. Just be aware that it's not right for everyone. I was on the Wiley Protocol for 7 or 8 months. It's relatively inexpensive (i.e., there's no $1900 sign-up fee, like with Dzugan). The hormones cost about $100-$150 a month, in addition to blood draws and doc appointments.
>
> Some things to be aware of: Wiley practitioners, if doing the protocol correctly, do blood draws to measure hormone levels rather than saliva. Measuring via saliva is often considered a more accurate measurement, as it quantifies the amount of hormone in the system that is free and available for the body to use. Blood tests may present a different picture but not clearly show how the body is using the hormones. See here for more info:
>
> [http://www.drmarcantel.com/hormone-testing-saliva-or-serum-blood-test/](http://www.drmarcantel.com/hormone-testing-saliva-or-serum-blood-test/)
>
> An additional issue I had was with the huge doses of hormones prescribed via the Wiley Protocol. After 6 or 7 months I became very bloated, I gained weight, my breasts increased 1-2 cup sizes (they're already too big), I was tired all the time, and had heart palpitations. My belly started protruding - it looked like I was 6 months pregnant. I continued to have vicious pre-menstrual mood swings. My periods got much weirder. My cycles would last 30-40 days total, and periods would last 10 days, including 1-2 days of obnoxiously heavy flow. My doc (who was also on Wiley) told me that sometimes she didn't have a period for months (she's around 40).
>
> All of these things (except the weight gain, unfortunately) resolved within a month of discontinuing the hormones. Now I'm back to a 27-28 day cycle, my belly isn't sticking out so far anymore (even though I haven't lost the weight), and my bras fit again. Please google "Progesterone toxicity symptoms." I had a lot of the symptoms. While on the protocol my labs indicated my progesterone levels were mid-range on Day 12 (0.6 with range 0.2-1.5) and the low end of the desired range for Day 21 (3.0 with range 1.7-27.0).
>
> TS Wiley is not a doctor - she has a BA in Anthropology, I think. Apparently some people feel great on this protocol, but just be aware that others have had problems.
>
> I hope that helps.

Interesting thread.

I would like to get some discussion/opinion on:

Saliva vs blood testing

is method of supplementation a factor of what should be tested

Progesterone toxicity

\-------------------

> This is same link as in Lanie's post.
>
> [http://www.drmarcantel.com/hormone-testing-saliva-or-serum-blood-test/](http://www.drmarcantel.com/hormone-testing-saliva-or-serum-blood-test/)
>
> Steroid hormones such as **estrogens, progesterone, testosterone, cortisol, and others are mostly bound to carrier proteins** in the bloodstream. It’s only the unbound 1-5% of these hormones that are actually available to target tissues and that actively exert specific effects on the body. Saliva tests, unlike serum (blood) tests, measure the level of these bioavailable hormones. Because of this, salivary hormone levels often relate to specific symptoms of hormone imbalances. A blood test, on the other hand, may show “normal” hormone levels but may not reveal how the hormones are actually affecting the body.

The way I am reading above,
it actually,
contradict conclusion,
that saliva testing provides better information.

I have example that is very strong for testosterone (but it applies to other steroid hormones)
Testosterone exist in the body in three places that it are measured via serum

As strongly bound to SHBG
As weakly bound to Albumin
As Free Testosterone

In the above we can hope that saliva testosterone levels are corresponding to FreeT in serum.
There is calculator
[http://www.issam.ch/freetesto.htm](http://www.issam.ch/freetesto.htm)
input SHBG, Albumin and Totaltestosterone
output ---->FreeTestosterone and BioAvailable Testosterone

You can see that FreeT is indeed very small % of total
It is so small
that it would not hurt much to just forget about it (figuratively speaking)
why
the testosterone that affect body is BioAvailableTestosterone BAT

BAT is combination of FreeT and testosterone that is weakly bound to Albumin

Grossly speaking

half of testosterone is bound to albumin and it is available at the moments notice

concentrating on just FreeT is like missing the boat by 50%
worrying if it is 2% or 2.5% while disregarding 50% elephant

\---------

Note in quotation above,
all hormones are bound to (Binding Proteins)
Some are bound tight some weakly.

Free form of hormones indeed is the part that does the job,
but
when that particular batch of Free hormone is used up, there is a truck load available in matter of seconds.

So, concentrating on just free form is not a very good reason as a defense of saliva testing (thinking out loud, I am not a doctor).

\----------------------
\----------------------

is method of supplementation a factor of what should be tested ?

Just to side step argumentation (saliva vs serum) (and get better overall service)
for my self I supplement with heaps of pregnenolone-MLM
I let the body sort out how much it wants to allocate for progesterone.

Note
using that method I am able to use blood test and actually track my progress and progesterone levels using serum.

Note#2
that was not the case when I would supplement with trans-dermal progesterone directly

Note#3
I am actually talking about two tablets of pregnenolone-MLM a day and I am able to get my progesterone up to 4.5 which is more that you got on day 21.

I am man, I should be happy with prog~1.4 (which is more than yours 0.6 on day 12)
reason I am going that high is because **dr Kruse wants (women) to go for P/E~500**

and when he argues the benefits of high progesterone levels, it does not looks like progesterone toxicity crosses his mind

\---------------------
\---------------------

In the last section you are saying things like
huge doses of hormones prescribed
Progesterone toxicity
and
(luckily) follow this statements with blood tests of your actual progesterone levels on that protocol

> While on the protocol my labs indicated my progesterone levels were mid-range
> on Day 12 (0.6 with range 0.2-1.5)
> and the low end of the desired range for
> Day 21 (3.0 with range 1.7-27.0).

My conclusion
those huge amounts of stuff
were not hormones
it was something else with possibly some traces of actual hormones.

So;
discussing
Progesterone toxicity
does not apply
while you are highly deficient of progesterone.

\--------------------------------------------------------------------------

There is always a possibility that you still can do even better.

# Thread: circadian disruption classified as carcinogen....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cantweight — 2013-05-10T18:57:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85794>

[http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/781058?src=wnl\_edit\_jrl&uac=98643BN](http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/781058?src=wnl_edit_jrl&uac=98643BN)

### nonchalant — 2013-05-10T19:02:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85796>

Can you quote a paragraph? I don't have a login.

### cantweight — 2013-05-10T19:38:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85798>

Have to do it in multiple posts...too long

Abstract
Objectives Animal evidence suggests that circadian disruption may be associated with ovarian cancer, though very little epidemiological work has been done to assess this potential association. We evaluated the association between self-reported nightshift work, a known circadian disruptor, and ovarian cancer in a population-based case-control study.
Methods The study included 1101 women with invasive epithelial ovarian cancer, 389 women with borderline epithelial ovarian tumours and 1832 controls and was conducted in western Washington state. Shift work data were collected as part of inperson interviews.
Results Working the nightshift was associated with an increased risk of invasive (OR=1.24, 95% CI 1.04 to 1.49) and borderline (OR=1.48, 95% CI 1.15 to 1.90) tumours; however, we observed little evidence that risks increased with increasing cumulative duration of nightshift work, and risks were not elevated in the highest duration category (>7 nightshift work-years). Increased risks were restricted to women who were 50 years of age and older and to serous and mucinous histologies of invasive and borderline tumours. There was suggestive evidence of a decreased risk of ovarian cancer among women reporting a preference for activity during evenings rather than mornings.
Conclusions We found evidence suggesting an association between shift work and ovarian cancer. This observation should be followed up in future studies incorporating detailed assessments of diurnal preference (ie, chronotype) in addition to detailed data on shift schedules.
Introduction
It is estimated that over 22 000 new cases of ovarian cancer will be diagnosed in the USA in 2012, with over 15 000 deaths from the disease.\[1\] There are few known factors that influence risk of ovarian cancer, including hormonal contraceptive use, parity and heavier body weight.\[2\] Given the high mortality rate among patients with ovarian cancer, the identification of potentially modifiable risk factors is crucial to disease prevention efforts.
Shift work resulting in circadian disruption has been classified as a probable human carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer.\[3\] Though the mechanisms behind the carcinogenicity of shift work are not fully understood, they are thought to be mediated by the indoleamine melatonin, which is produced at night-time and suppressed by ambient light.\[4\] Melatonin is a regulator of the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis, and the effect of melatonin on reproductive hormones, including oestrogen, has been considered a primary mechanism by which shift work may be associated with breast cancer risk.\[5\]

This mechanism may also extend to ovarian cancer for which there is evidence that endogenous reproductive hormones are involved in the pathogenesis of the disease.\[6\] Evidence suggests that melatonin may be directly involved in ovarian function, including follicular development, ovulation, oocyte maturation and luteal function.\[7\] Extensive animal and cellular experimental data have shown melatonin to have direct oncostatic properties which may be mediated through an effect on reactive oxygen species.\[8\] In fact, in a study of rats, oral administration of melatonin was shown to decrease lipid hydroperoxide levels, increase total levels of antioxidant substances and increase the activity of antioxidant enzymes, including superoxide dismutase, catalase and glutathione-reductase in ovarian tissue.\[9\]
Despite this suggestive experimental evidence, few epidemiological studies have examined the association between shift work and ovarian cancer. Here we describe a large population-based case-control study in which we examined the relation of lifetime nightshift work with risk of epithelial ovarian tumours.
Materials and Methods
Study Population
Female residents of a 13-county area of western Washington state who were diagnosed with a primary invasive or borderline epithelial ovarian tumour from 2002 to 2009 were considered eligible as cases. From 2002 to 2005, women aged 35–74 years were included, while from 2006 to 2009 only women aged 35–69 years were included. The cases were identified through a population-based cancer registry, the Cancer Surveillance System (CSS), which is part of the Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results Program of the US National Cancer Institute. The methods for case ascertainment and for case and control recruitment for 2002–2005 have been previously described;\[10\] similar methods were used for 2006–2009.\[11\] After excluding 61 cases because of a potential language barrier, 2025 eligible cases were identified, and 1502 (74.2%) were interviewed. Of the interviewed cases, 1108 had invasive disease and 394 had borderline tumours. Histological type was collected and coded by the CSS using the International Classification of Diseases for Oncology\[12\] morphology codes and grouped according to the guidelines of WHO\[13\] as serous, mucinous, endometrioid, clear cell and other epithelial tumours (comprised largely of unspecified adenocarcinoma and carcinoma). Tumour grade was also collected by the CSS as grades 1 (well differentiated) through 4 (undifferentiated) or unknown. In addition to assessing risk of invasive and borderline tumours separately, we created the following additional analytic groupings of invasive cancers, based on a recent conceptualisation of ovarian cancer pathogenesis:\[14 15\] high-grade serous (HGSC; comprised of serous morphology, grades 2–4 or unknown, as well as grade 3 or higher tumours that were morphologically classified as endometrioid; n=840); low-grade serous (LGSC; grade 1 serous; n=23); mucinous (MC; n=32); endometrioid (EC; morphologically endometrioid and grade 1, 2 or unknown; n=129); and clear cell tumours (CCC; n=84). Based on molecular similarities observed between (1) borderline serous tumours and invasive LGSC and (2) borderline and invasive mucinous tumours, we further combined invasive and borderline tumours of these subtypes. Other borderline tumours (n=23) were excluded from the subtype analyses.
Controls were selected by random-digit dialling (RDD) using stratified sampling in 5-year age categories, 1-year calendar intervals and two county strata. From 2002 to 2005, a 2:1 ratio of controls to women with invasive ovarian cancer was selected using the Waksberg–Mitofsky RDD method.\[16\] From 2006 to 2009, a 1:1 ratio of controls to women with invasive disease was used; list-assisted RDD\[17\] was employed during 2006–2007 and Waksberg–Mitofsky methods during 2008–2009. In total, for 19 092 (78.2%) of the 24 400 telephone numbers belonging to residences, we determined whether an eligible woman (ie, an age and county eligible woman able to communicate in English and, if so, with at least one ovary and no prior history of ovarian cancer) resided there. Of the 2351 eligible women identified, 1849 were interviewed (78.6%). The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center, and all women provided signed informed consent before participating.

### cantweight — 2013-05-10T19:39:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85799>

Data Collection
Inperson interviews pertained to the period of time before diagnosis (for cases) or before an assigned comparable reference date (for controls), and covered the following: demographic and lifestyle characteristics; employment history; medical history; and detailed reproductive history, including menstrual, pregnancy, and use of contraceptive and menopausal hormone preparations.
In addition to start and end dates and average hours per week for each job held at least a continuous 4 months starting at age 25 until the reference date, participants were asked to report how many of their work days over the duration of the job included the hours between midnight and 04:00 (nightshift), to which they could respond: never worked those hours; less than half the days; at least half the days; or every work day. As a measure of chronotype (preference for being active in the morning or the evening), which may be associated with the ability to adapt to nightshift work, participants were asked to report if they were morning persons, evening persons or other (assessed as of 5 years before the reference date).
Shift Work Variables
For the primary analysis, two shift work variables were created: ever/never worked any nightshift and cumulative nightshift work-years (from age 25 until the reference date). For each participant with j jobs, cumulative nightshift work-years (CN) was calculated as follows:

where Dj is the total number of months engaged in a particular job, Hj is the hours worked per week for that job and Fj is the fraction of time spent doing nightshift work for that job. For each job, Fj was assigned as 0 if the subject reported that she never worked nightshifts, 0.25 if she reported that less than half of the days involved nightshifts, 0.5 if she reported that at least half of the days involved nightshifts or 1.0 if she reported that every work day involved nightshifts. Work-years were calculated by dividing the total number of hours engaged in nightshift work for a particular job by 2080, which is the total number of hours worked in a year assuming an average of 40 h of work per week.
We also examined ever/never worked in a job with less than half of work days doing the nightshift and ever/never worked a job with all nightshifts. These categorisations were not mutually exclusive, that is, a participant reporting working one or more jobs with less than half of works days doing the nightshift and one or more jobs with all nightshifts would be in the 'ever' exposed category in both analyses.
Statistical Analysis
Unconditional logistic regression was used to calculate ORs and corresponding 95% CI. All analyses were adjusted for the frequency-matching variables of age at reference (5-year intervals), county of residence (dichotomised as the three urban or 10 rural/suburban counties in the study) and calendar year of diagnosis/reference date (continuous). We also adjusted for factors known to be associated with ovarian cancer: duration of hormonal contraceptive use (never, <6, 6–59, 60–119 or ≥120 months), number of full term pregnancies (0, 1, 2 or ≥3) and body mass index (BMI) at age 30 (<18.5, 18.5–24.9, 25–29.9 or ≥30). Adjustment for additional factors including race/ethnicity, history of hormone replacement therapy, smoking status, alcohol use and education had a minimal impact (ie, < 10% change) on point estimates of interest, so these variables were not included in the final analyses. Cumulative nightshift work-years were categorised according to approximate quartiles among controls who were ever engaged in nightshift work. Median number of work-years was also compared between cases and controls using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test given the highly skewed distribution of the variable.
Additional separate analyses were conducted to examine the association of shift work with risk of ovarian cancer among women diagnosed before and after age 50 years and to examine the association of shift work with tumour subtypes (as described above). We also evaluated whether chronotype (morning or evening preference) modified the association between shift work and ovarian cancer. Participants who did not indicate a morning or evening preference were excluded from this analysis. Effect modification was formally evaluated using likelihood ratio tests comparing models with and without chronotype and nightshift work cross-products terms. All analyses were carried out using the STATA V.11 statistical package (Statacorp LP, College Station, Texas, USA).

### cantweight — 2013-05-10T19:40:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85800>

Results
After excluding 29 subjects with missing covariate data on BMI, number of births or duration of hormonal contraception, a total of 1101 invasive cancer cases, 389 borderline tumour cases and 1832 controls were included in our analyses. The distribution of various characteristics of cases and controls is provided in Table 1. Compared with controls, a lesser proportion of invasive cases used hormonal contraceptives, and invasive and borderline cases tended to have had fewer children than controls. As expected, women with borderline tumours tended to be younger than women with invasive disease.
Among the 27% of invasive cases that reported ever working a night job (Table 2), the median number of work-years engaged in the nightshift was 3.5, and among the 32% of borderline cases, the median number of work-years engaged in the nightshift was 3.2. Among the 23% of controls working night jobs, the median number of work-years engaged in the nightshift was 2.7; these differences between the case groups and controls were not statistically significant (p>0.5). Among controls and invasive tumour cases, the majority of jobs that were reported as involving nightshift work were in healthcare (27% and 28%, respectively), mostly nursing, followed by food preparation and service (eg, cooks, waitresses, bartenders) (18% and 16%) and office and administrative support (eg, telephone operators, customer service representatives, dispatchers) (17% and 15%). Among borderline cases, the majority of nightshift jobs were in food preparation and service (24%), followed by healthcare (20%) and office and administrative support (13%).
Ever working the nightshift was associated with a 1.24-fold increased risk of invasive epithelial ovarian cancer (95% CI 1.04 to 1.49) and a 1.48-fold increased risk of borderline epithelial ovarian tumours (95% CI 1.15 to 1.90). When examining cumulative nightshift work-years, there were no indications of a trend with either invasive or borderline tumours; significant increases in risk were restricted to the second highest cumulative category (>3–7 work-years).
In additional analyses, risks of invasive and borderline tumours among women who reported ever working a job with less than half of the work days as nights (ORinvasive=1.28, 95% CI 1.03 to 1.59; ORborderline=1.32, 95% CI 0.98 to 1.78) and who reported working a job with all nights (ORinvasive=1.16, 95% CI 0.87 to 1.55; ORborderline=1.29, 95% CI 0.87 to 1.92) were not materially different from those observed in the main analyses evaluating any history of ever working the nightshift (Table 2). A greater percentage of evening type individuals had ever engaged in nightshift work than had morning types (27% vs 20% of controls; χ2, p=0.002); while the risks of invasive and borderline ovarian tumours for those ever working the nightshift among self-classified morning type individuals (ORinvasive=1.29, 95% CI 1.00 to 1.67; ORborderline=1.57, 95% CI 1.08 to 2.27) was slightly greater than those ever working the nightshift among self-classified evening type individuals (ORinvasive=1.14, 95% CI 0.85 to 1.53; ORborderline=1.43, 95% CI 0.97 to 2.09), the differential effects were not statistically significant (pinvasive=0.47; pborderline=0.54).

When stratifying by reference age, ever working the nightshift was significantly associated with increased risks of both invasive and borderline tumours among women who were 50 years of age or older (Table 3), but risks were not significantly elevated in women under 50 years of age. These apparent differential affects by age were not statistically significant (pinvasive=0.32; pborderline=0.65). Table 4 presents the results of the subtype-specific analyses of invasive and borderline disease. Increased risks of HGSC and LGSC (the latter including borderline and invasive disease) were associated with ever working the nightshift (ORs=1.29, 95% CI 1.06 to 1.57, and 1.51, 95% CI 1.12 to 2.05, respectively). Risk of MC (including invasive and borderline disease) was also positively associated with ever working the nightshift (OR=1.55, 95% CI 1.10 to 2.17). In contrast, overall risks of both EC and CCC were, if anything, slightly reduced in association with ever working the nightshift (OR=0.91 for each of these subtypes). To assess whether subtype-specific differences were simply reflecting an age effect or vice versa, we conducted age-specific analyses within histological subgroups. No clear differences in risk by age within the EC, CCC, MC and LGSC subgroups were observed, while risks among the most common subtype, HGSC, were primarily increased among women aged 50 or more years (data not shown)
Discussion
In our large population-based case-control study, we found evidence suggesting an association between ever working the nightshift and increased risk of epithelial ovarian tumours. The increased risks that we observed are consistent in magnitude with those observed for breast cancer in previous studies.\[18\] However, we did not observe a trend with increasing cumulative duration of nightshift work nor did we observe an increased risk in the highest category of cumulative duration. There was suggestive evidence that increased risks were restricted to women 50 years of age and older. In tumour subtype analyses, elevated risks associated with nightshift work were observed for HGSC, LGSC and MC subtypes (the last two including both invasive and borderline tumours), but not with EC or CCC. While risks associated with most reproductive factors have been consistent across all histological types,\[19\] associations with certain factors such as smoking, endometriosis and BMI have been observed to vary by histology.\[19–21\]
Chronotype has been considered by others as a measure of adaptability to shift work schedules, with evening type individuals reporting better tolerance to nightshift work (eg, better work performance and higher job satisfaction) than morning type individuals.\[22\] In the current study, a greater proportion of evening type individuals among our controls had a history of nightshift work, but there was only suggestive evidence that ovarian cancer was elevated to a lesser extent among such individuals than among morning type persons.
In a recent prospective analysis of 718 ovarian cancer cases among 181 548 participants in the Nurse's Health Study, there was no evidence of an association between rotating shift work and ovarian cancer risk.\[23\] Rotating shift work was defined as working at least three nights per month in addition to day or evening shifts and has been previously associated with breast, endometrial and colorectal cancers in the cohort.\[24–26\] The study did not collect data on permanent/fixed nightshift work. Though our study did not specifically ascertain data on rotating shift work, it is reasonable to assume a rotating shift work schedule for those reporting working jobs with less than half of the work days involving the nightshift. We found evidence of increased risks of invasive ovarian cancer in association with nightshift work even when restricting analyses to this group. It is difficult to directly compare the results of our study with those of the Nurse's Health Study given the differences in shift work data that were collected.

### cantweight — 2013-05-10T19:41:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85801>

Animal and laboratory data suggest that an increased risk of ovarian cancer associated with circadian disruption may be plausible. In turkey breeder hens with ovarian adenocarcinomas, longer day periods (ie, longer exposure to light) promoted growth of ovarian tumours, while shorter day periods were found to inhibit growth.\[27\] Direct administration of melatonin was also found to inhibit tumour growth. As has been hypothesised for breast cancer, melatonin may impact ovarian cancer risk through an effect on endogenous levels of reproductive hormones, particularly oestrogen. Though evidence suggests that oestrogens may be especially important in the aetiology of EC and CCC,\[6\] we did not observe associations between nightshift work and risk of these subtypes. Beyond its effects on reproductive hormones, melatonin has been shown to have direct oncostatic properties. In addition to directly scavenging reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, melatonin stimulates other antioxidants and enzymes that metabolise reactive species.\[7\] In a study of female rats, ethanol was found to increase lipid hydroperoxide levels in ovarian tissue; with concurrent melatonin administration, lipid hydroperoxide levels were significantly decreased, and the antioxidant activities of superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase and glutathione reductase were all increased.\[28\]
The large size of our study and comprehensive data on historic nightshift schedules, as well as important potential confounders, are strengths. Though participation rates were relatively high for both cases and controls, participation bias may have influenced study results if those women who chose not to participate in our study differed from study subjects in factors related to nightshift work. Although data on nightshift work were retrospectively collected through self-report, the potential association of shift work with ovarian cancer was not a primary hypothesis of the study that was expressed to participants, so recall bias is unlikely. Further, while study participants may have been aware of light-at-night as a hypothesised risk factor for other cancers, little or no attention had been paid to ovarian cancer during the years in which our study was conducted. The prevalence of shift work in our study population is higher than what has been reported for the general US working population by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) (17.7% among US salaried workers).\[29\] However, the higher prevalence is consistent with the types of jobs that study subjects reported working. For example, among controls, the most commonly worked jobs involving the nightshift were in the healthcare industry, and according to the BLS, over 24% of salaried healthcare workers in the US were engaged in shift work in 2004. Food preparation and service was the next most common class of job involving nightshift work that controls reported. According to BLS data, over 49% of salaried US food preparation and service workers were engaged in shift work in 2004.
We did not observe a significant dose–response relationship with cumulative nightshift work-years, which may be attributable to exposure misclassification. For instance, the fraction of time spent working the nightshift for a particular job could, in actuality, be any value between 0 and 1, but for this study, we were only able to assign one of four values to this fraction. Also, information on intensity of nightshift schedules was not ascertained as part of this study, meaning that a participant working a full-time job with two nights per week over a period of 10 years would be treated the same in our analyses as a participant working the nightshift for the first 2 years of a full-time job that lasted for 10 years. In addition, the exposure variables, while relatively detailed, may not have captured the aspect of exposure most linked with risk. For example, with rotating shift work schedules, the rate of rotation and direction of rotation may be particularly important,\[30\] but that information was not collected as part of this study. We did, however, have data on chronotype, which may be a critical modifier of the association between nightshift work and cancer but mostly has not been evaluated in studies of nightshift work and cancer risk. However, this variable was based on a response to a single question, and there are much more comprehensive assessment tools available, such as the Circadian Type Inventory (18-item questionnaire), which assign scores to classify individuals on a morningness–eveningness scale.\[31\] Chance must also be considered as an explanation of our findings.

We found suggestive evidence that nightshift work is associated with increased risks of both invasive and borderline ovarian tumours, particularly of serous and mucinous subtypes and among women who are 50 years of age and older. However, our results contrast with those of the single prior study of ovarian cancer risk that evaluated rotating shift work. Further research is needed, and future studies should incorporate assessments of chronotype to further explore whether evening-type persons are at a decreased risk of developing ovarian cancer as a result of better adaptability to shift work schedules. These studies should also evaluate the impact of genetic variation in circadian genes in conjunction with chronotype on susceptibility to the potential carcinogenic effects of nightshift work. Prospectively designed studies are likely to provide the best option for collecting the detailed high-quality shift work data that are needed. However, for rare diseases such as ovarian cancer, individual cohorts may lack power to detect significant associations, particularly with respect to stratification on chronotype, genetic variation or tumour morphology

# Thread: Thyroid Lab Questions, Please Help!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-lab-questions-please-help.7015/>

### saben — 2013-05-11T02:02:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-lab-questions-please-help.7015/post-85816>

Hi,
I have Hashimoto's and have been working to feel better-non-flouridated water, Blue-light blocking glasses at night, EMF mitigation, diet, progesterone, DHEA, Mitochondrial RX...I've seen progress but still struggling with energy. I'm currently on 1 grain of Naturethroid and 5 mcg of Cytomel. I doubled my dose of both to see if I felt better and I think I do. I checked in with my doctor who did not feel comfortable increasing my dose of either medication without checking my current thyroid functioning. Just received an email with my results:
T3, Free 2.6 Range 2.3-4.2 pg/ml
TSH 0.9 Range .40-4.50 miu/l
T4, Free (Thyroxine Free Direct) 0.9 Range .8-1.8 ng/dl, with a note saying the current lot of Free T4 reagent available from the manufacturer produce results that are approximately 9% higher than previous reagent lots. Please interpret these results accordingly.

What are your thoughts on my thyroid function? What would be good numbers for labs? My doctor seems to think my results are normal, but I still feel better on a double dose of both medications. Which medication would be more important for optimal labs and feeling optimal?

Thanks.

To see if you are using the T4 contained in Naturethroid you would have to know your RT3 and your inflammation status.

Having hashi (autoimmune problem)
it is good idea to not eat nightshades.

Are you man or woman?

Basically either should not use progesterone but specially men.

Progesterone is very important hormone and you want a lots of it
but
exhaust first potential of

pregnenolone and DHEA supplementation.

I am partial to pills of
pregnenolone-MLM
and
DHEA-MLM

\-------

Before supplementing with thyroid hormones directly
it is good idea to prepare background
so
thyroid hormones may do their good work.

As a minimum good cortisol-AM

but also iodine and all micronutrients that can be tested using Spectracell.

FYI,

For those suffering thyroid problems, this is an extremely valuable website & book:

Also, thyroid problems are the result of iodine deficiency. Many with thyroid problems fully recover when taking the Dr. Brownstein Iodine Protocol. Details here:
[http://tinyurl.com/Iodine-Handout](http://tinyurl.com/Iodine-Handout)

For women, the thyroid, uterus, ovaries, brain & breasts battle over what little iodine you get in your diet, and one or more of these organs gets shorted resulting in disease... typically painful lumps & bumps in the breasts, PCOS, uterus problems or thyroid problems. This is the reason why women suffer from thyroid problems far more then men do and at earlier ages. Thyroid is usually the first to go from iodine deficiency because it is so highly dependent on iodine. Symptoms are swollen thyroid, nodules, hypothyroid and for those who are also grossly deficient in selenium - hyperthyroid. Doctors should be treating thyroid problems with iodine, but they won't because it will destroy their income. The thyroid can get permanently damaged from iodine deficiency, and in these cases, thyroid meds may be needed for life. Yet the need for iodine is still present for the brain, breasts, uterus & ovaries. Common symptoms of iodine deficiency is Brain Fog, headaches, & feeling stupid. A [great new book on this subject](http://www.amazon.com/The-Iodine-Crisis-About-Wreck/dp/098603200X) is **"The Iodine Crises: What you don't know about iodine can ruin your life."**

Men also suffer from iodine deficiency, but not nearly so much as women, and much later in life. The brain fog, & thyroid problems are similar for men, followed by prostate & testes problems. In men, libido, hormonal & dysfunction problems fade away with iodine supplements.

List of iodine deficiency symptoms:
[http://tinyurl.com/Iodine-Deficiency](http://tinyurl.com/Iodine-Deficiency)

Please join with us at the [iodine thread,](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?943-The-iodine-Thread!!!/page57)

### Martin — 2013-05-11T23:59:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-specifics-on-buying-an-ac.7021/post-85858>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=85851)
>
> I am also interested in this EMF type of question.
> But I also have another AC/heat related question.
>
> AC when it works produces lots of condensate (water).
> Mostly during winter months air is dry and humidifier kicks in (another water)
> All is well when all is working as it should.

> But we have example from Martin, about what can happen when there is a problem that may be hard to see and it persist for period of time.
> Martin suffers from exposure to black mold.
>
> It would be nice to discuss ways to avoid this.
>
>
> ....

We opted not to have humidifier on our system and I'm glad we did. It was a money issue at the time.

I wonder how the humidifying systems _really_ work after having all these problems with new equipment.

Some easy things are visual inspections of the unit downwind of the "condensing coil", the gizmo located in the ductwork system where the copper and black foam rubber covered refrigerant tubes go into the side of the metal cabinet. There is an inspection cover with screws and it's easy to remove and shine a light into and inspect.

Before I discovered the leaking, I was just checking the drain that drips water from the AC to the outside, and while it was still flowing, I figured it was all right. Turns ou the drip pan was warped and the water was going out two ways - dripping into my ducts, and out the drain. Fooled!

I noticed my problem when I finally got around to crawling under the house and saw/heard it was dripping water onto the ground below it. There was the catch pan, the one that failed. Also, we chose fiberglass duct board material instead of metal to deaden sound and provide insulation, and, a little cheaper. Turns out duct board is a fabo mold growing medium when soaked with water. Replaced all ducting with metal, louder, but safer.

Inspection schedule is key. Just because it's _new_ don't mean it ain't going bonkers...

There is an inspection cover with screws and it's easy to remove and shine a light into and inspect.
>
> Before I discovered the leaking, I was just checking the drain that drips water from the AC to the outside, and while it was still flowing, I figured it was all right. Turns ou the drip pan was warped and the water was going out two ways - dripping into my ducts, and out the drain. Fooled!
>
> I noticed my problem when I finally got around to crawling under the house and saw/heard it was dripping water onto the ground below it. There was the catch pan, the one that failed. Also, we chose fiberglass duct board material instead of metal to deaden sound and provide insulation, and, a little cheaper. Turns out duct board is a fabo mold growing medium when soaked with water. Replaced all ducting with metal, louder, but safer.
>
> Inspection schedule is key. Just because it's _new_ don't mean it ain't going bonkers...

OMGosh..wonder if this is happening at our house. Though we have no way to crawl underneath.

He can now speed read and writes mostly with Dragon Speech. He built a plane that flew, but broke up with him in it 25 feet off the ground when he was 9. One day he was driving across East Texas and he got caught in a tornado. He found himself holding onto a tree inside the tornado and he said the behavior of the water inside the tornado awed him to the point that he decided to study water. Reading Pollack's book, I am getting an idea of what the water may have looked like inside the tornado and inside our nano tubules of the mitochondria...For me this is a breakthrough in picturing what might be going on. The diagrams are helping me turn on my thinking in pictures brain to mock things up and test them. There are also pictures of many of the people we discuss such as Ling and Mae Wan Ho which makes them more real for me. I know I can use Google images, and the book is more personal. My water friend says his water world friends have been happy that the book was published because now they have something that helps them talk about their work. Better late than never....

### yewwei.tan — 2013-11-22T11:43:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee.7032/post-101328>

Dave Asprey's take on coffee is definitely optimised toward ensuring a toxin-free brew, and he's even admitted that his decaf is pretty lacking on the taste department as a result. I've never tried his coffee though.

Regarding MCT oil, coconut oil is just MCT + some other types of fat. ie: MCTs are a part of coconut oil. Just think of pure MCT as concentrated coconut oil (6 times stronger in regular MCT). So you could say that you need to consume 6 times less MCT to achieve the same ketogenic effect as coconut oil. So you should add MCT if you're after the extra brain-boosting effects.

Relatedly, Ben Greenfield recently did [a podcast](http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com/2013/11/263-5-ways-to-stay-awake-without-coffee-why-you-shouldnt-mix-carbs-and-fats-how-to-build-extreme-speed/) (with [a transcript here](http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com/2013/11/episode-263-full-transcript/)) talking about "5 ways to stay awake without coffee", whereby he mentions decaf vs caffeinated coffee. Greenfield essentially does caffeinated coffee for 6 weeks, and then decaf for 2 weeks to re-sensitise himself back to the effects of caffeine as well as to mitigate some of the negative effects. He also talks about how green tea contains L-tyrosine, which gives your brain a boost, but without the sleep disruption (so Inger may be correct to grab the tea instead!)

Personally, no coffee for me, since I didn't like where my BUN/Creatinine ratio was going. I ended up limiting most teas pretty heavily as well, and ended up doing bitter melon tea as a substitute.

Sidenote for Aussies: Nature Pacific Coconut oil from Fiji is amazing! Got myself a 20L pail a month ago for $228, or $11.40/L (that's inclusive of shipping costs from Queenslands) -- [http://www.ebay.com.au/itm/CERTIFIE...98?pt=LH\_DefaultDomain\_15&hash=item27d116107a](http://www.ebay.com.au/itm/CERTIFIED-ORGANIC-Extra-Virgin-Coconut-Oil-20-litres-/171011608698?pt=LH_DefaultDomain_15&hash=item27d116107a)

### caroline — 2013-11-23T06:21:34+0200

### yewwei.tan — 2013-11-23T10:17:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee.7032/post-101461>

@caroline -- Don't know why I never thought of that. I buy so many things from iherb, but never took a look at coconut oil. I should give it a shot pretty soon.

@jude -- the breakdown of 100g of coconut oil is as follows:

1\. In 100g of coconut oil, there is about 90g of saturated fat, with the other 10g being mono and polyunsaturated fat

2\. Of that 90g of saturated fat, about 65g of that are medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs)

3\. Of that 65g of MCTs, only about 15g are caprylic acid (C8) and capric acid (C10). These are thought to be the "most ketogenic" MCTs

4\. A good MCT oil only consists of C8 and C10. This is where the "6x more powerful than coconut oil" claim comes from -- it would take 100g of coconut oil to get "the same ketogenic effect" as 15g of pure MCT oil.

5\. Between C8 and C10, C8 is "more ketogenic". There is usually only about 5-7g of C8 in coconut oil.

6\. Dave Asprey sells this thing called 'Brain Octane' which is nothing but C8, which is 1/18th the composition of coconut oil, and hence he claims that it's "18x more potent than coconut oil"

There are benefits to having to take less of an oil:
\- Less chance of diarrhea due to MCT overconsumption
\- Easier compliance for some folks

Personally, I can take 4 tablespoons of pure MCT and do just fine

Hope that helps!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee.7032/post-101467>

### yewwei.tan — 2013-11-24T12:31:53+0200

> [MeghanK said:](/goto/post?id=101519)

>
> **What does coffee and tea have to do with BUN/Creatinine....
> I LOVE LOOSE LEAF TEA and just started to drink some coffee mayb 2-3x a week for the antioxidants and other benefits... I add a little vanilla (not powder, just pure vanilla) and about 1/8 to 1/16 of stevia... is stevia bad to add? Is powdered vanilla better than the liquid?**

Yup, coffee and tea have a ton of polyphenols, the caffeine is probably good for you, so do drink them if you can.

Nothing wrong with stevia either as far as I know.

Dr Kruse talked about the issue with coffee here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthre...fee-Leptin-Reset&p=95413&viewfull=1#post95413](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?8183-Bulletproof-Optimal-Coffee-Leptin-Reset&p=95413&viewfull=1#post95413) . After reading EE3 and looking at my labs, I decided I needed all the magnesium I could get, and coffee is just one of many things that could deplete magnesium further.
