# Thread: Bluefish ceviche
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bluefish-ceviche.7160/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2013-05-26T05:48:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bluefish-ceviche.7160/post-86876>

Ingredients: 1 ocean full of bluefish.
Balsamic vinegar or lemon juice
Salt and pepper
Prep: Drive to the south shore of Long Island with a surfcasting rod 2 hrs before sunset.
Wade into the water barefoot up to your waist (water temp 50f)
Catch as many bluefish as you can before sun sets (2)
Fillet bluefish immediately. cut into bite size pieces. Toss with Balsamic or lemon ,salt, and pepper.
Watch sunset while you sit on beach and eat fish.

I had this dish the other night and recommend it highly.

### Inger — 2013-05-26T07:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bluefish-ceviche.7160/post-86880>

Oh man.. can I come with you and eat? This sounds like heaven to me.. so fresh.. so simple. Now, this is food that makes me feel amazing!

### caroline — 2013-05-26T08:33:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bluefish-ceviche.7160/post-86883>

It does indeed sound like heaven .....appreciating the simple, wonderful things in life ...can't get any better if you ask me.....

### Shijin13 — 2013-05-26T16:31:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bluefish-ceviche.7160/post-86888>

Dali

I'm sharing this recipe w/DH he love bluefish and its really cheap at our local international market...

# Thread: Some research on meditation/mindfulness and telomeres
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-research-on-meditation-mindfulness-and-telomeres.7161/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-05-26T22:25:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-research-on-meditation-mindfulness-and-telomeres.7161/post-86895>

I just came across some intriguing information through one of Dave Asprey's blogs, phase2minds.com. [http://mindbrain.ucdavis.edu/labs/Saron/pdf/Jacobs et al 2010 Psychoneuroendocrinology.pdf](http://mindbrain.ucdavis.edu/labs/Saron/pdf/Jacobs%20et%20al%202010%20Psychoneuroendocrinology.pdf)

There are a lot of additional cites within this study that seem interesting as well, and when I come across something interesting like this I normally look into everything cited as well. I don't have the time for that today, unfortunately, need to finish up this module and complete a genetics exam then start applying to dozens of places for a new job so I can start buying more/higher quality fish, lol! From what I understand from **briefly** skimming this, is that stress, depression, psychological problems and trauma can affect telomeres.... BUT so can eustress and 'using' stress, so can meditation, mindfulness, and gratitude. This is really getting me excited to start meditating again, although I really enjoy it more from a spiritual and psychological point of view.

As indicated by one of my recent posts, I was looking for the "best epi-paleo bang for my buck" since I'm terribly low on cash right now - I think I meditation may have to be included in there. I still have anxiety problems as it is (much better than 15 months ago), and sometimes I'm not so confidant in the control of my thoughts - meditation should help. By the way, does anyone have any insights on some things I've experienced/have been experiencing? Sometimes when I lay down to sleep, typically only when I lay on my back, right as I start drifting off it literally feels as if I'm going to 'pop out of body' and have an out of body experience. I've read up on OBE's and astral projection, and I know it can be achieved, whether it is something biological, something happening on the quantum level, or spiritually, I don't know, but it unfortunately frightens me at night. I get vibrations and a sudden airy/light feeling, I feel as though I don't weigh a single pound and that my consciousness is literally going to pop out of my body. This started after I got back into lucid dreaming recently. I also experience some odd sensations such as these once and awhile when meditating or listening to binaural audio... any insight would be helpful, as would any meditation techniques offering more of a calming effect. Thanks! Hope you enjoy the study!

### Inger — 2013-05-27T09:11:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-research-on-meditation-mindfulness-and-telomeres.7161/post-86934>

BPC777,
IDK but I personally am careful with "weird" meditations.. I do not do them. I have seen where this stuff have lead my brother and others and that is not a place I want to go. I do other stuff, like I cut out my thoughts and just concentrate on listening.. to all the different sounds around me, 100%. Or I concentrate on scents.. only that. Or I just look.. at the sky.. at the green trees and flowers.. I just am there and think nothing, I just enjoy the present. Very close to earth, nothing esoteric. To me the reality is very important. I can take a cup.. touch it and look at its color.. and just say to myself, this is a cup. It is white. And I touch it and feel it and it feels so good. It is so real. I really need reality because of all the weird religiosity I was raised in.. it was making me nauseous. Maybe try to go outside, sit on the ground or stay with bare feet, and just suck in the nature.. its sounds.. and scents.. anything. Makes my mind so very relaxed! Singing is great too.. it helps mood issues a lot! I sing so much alone..lol I put in so much feeling and it is very healing and makes me happy!

Coffee makes me anxious if I have too much or am stressed... not good at all. More than one cup and I get to feel it...

# Thread: Losing my poison ivy allergy?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/losing-my-poison-ivy-allergy.7163/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2013-05-27T02:59:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/losing-my-poison-ivy-allergy.7163/post-86914>

The ivy was slowly creeping up my porch; I knew I had to deal with it. But I hadn't wanted to get any of my garden tools infected with the horrid sap. I was sitting, staring at it a week ago, and on impulse I reached over (carefully using my left hand) and plucked it. What courage! How decisive! Was I nutz?? I have to avoid gloves that may have touched poison ivy long ago, or risk hives. Forget deliberately touching it! But I have noticed, over the past year, a reduced allergy reaction to lots of my previous triggers -- foods, cats, pollen, etc etc etc.

After about 10 minutes I lost courage, and rinsed my hand with water. No soap, however. The next day, I was just fine. I couldn't believe it! That morning, I deliberately walked through a patch of young (short) poison ivy with just my flip flops on. And no washing, until that night. Ok, the next day I did have a watery pimple thingie, on the top of my left foot. And my right hand also had one, near the site of a bromine bump. But that's it!!!!! And I'm fine now, those two pimples are healed. No red swollen patches of skin, no unbearable itching. And I can tell poison ivy from Virginia Creeper or other plants in an instant.

This is amazing to me. I had no idea that one could become un-allergic to this scourge. Was it CT? Seafood? Water? Grounding? Thanks, Jack!

### Inger — 2013-05-27T08:09:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/losing-my-poison-ivy-allergy.7163/post-86929>

How amazing Sally...!!! I am totally confident when the body heals we lose every single allergy! I think it is all the stuff together that makes a big difference..  this is such a wonderful road to travel, full of nice surprises  It just gets better and better!

Now when thinking about it.. I used to itch in the night from stinging nettles.. so it disrupted my sleep. I stepped on stinging nettles as I did not see them in the pasture a week or so ago.. I thought.. argh! now I lose my sleep this night for sure! But my feet didn't itch at all that night, I forgot about the whole thing! I am curious to see if I want itch at all this summer.. I always step on those bastards running around here barefeet. I do not mind the minor pain but the itching in the night was a nuisance. Maybe I am healing too like you!  And Sally.. my second bromide bump is fully healed by now too. But I have been careful not taking any Iodine.. and have also cut back on the seaweeds too. Just taking a little. Now I need to increase their use again me thinks.

### nonchalant — 2013-05-27T16:05:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/losing-my-poison-ivy-allergy.7163/post-86940>

Inger, my bromide bump is still with me, but I very seldom put iodine on my skin. I either don't think about it, or I'm going to be visiting people, and don't want strange dark marks on my skin. Vanity! I have put iodine on the bump a couple times, and it did reduce in size. That's it, I'll go put some on right now.

# Thread: ? on preg/dhea supplementation and sleep/cold intolerance
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-preg-dhea-supplementation-and-sleep-cold-intolerance.7164/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bennyha — 2013-05-27T17:16:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-preg-dhea-supplementation-and-sleep-cold-intolerance.7164/post-86944>

Hello all. I have another question I was hoping you fine members could help me with. I started taking preg and dhea on my own last week, and upped iodine to 50 mg. On my last saliva cortisol draw, I was around midrange with morning cortisol and midrange with dhea. Also have low body temps and pulse rates (as low as 95 degrees some days with 40 beats per minute heart rate measured last week). I am taking these hoping the preg to increase my morning cortisol.

I have been taking 150 mg preg and 50 mg dhea at 6 a.m and again at 10 a.m. for the past 5 days. I have also in this time upped the iodine to 50 mg total (25 mg @ breakfast and 25 mg @ lunch and taking required co-factors as well). I have noticed a few good things, such as less brain fog, a little more energy, and my temperatures in the morning are 98.6 and my pulse has been 66 beats per min the past 3 mornings. This is great as sometimes my pulse is as low as 40 bpm last week.

On the other side, I have been experiencing extreme sensitivity to coldness. My hands/feet/toes are ice cold all day long. They feel as if I have been submerged in ice for hours they are that cold to the touch. Also, my sleep had been good, sleeping from 10 pm to 5:30 a.m. each morning and still waking multiple times to pee. A few questions.

1\. Why do I have the sudden increase in extreme cold sensitivity to my hands/feet/nose? Is it just because my T3 is flatlined with a very high TSH (so basically "hypothyroidism" using standard definition)? Or is this another sign of adrenal fatigue?

2\. Why is my sleep returning to my adrenal fatigue days? I was getting up each day with the sun naturally (around 5:30 each morning since summer has arrived) but the past few days I have been getting up at 4:00 a.m. and feel wide awake and cannot get back to sleep for anything. Is the increase in preg and dhea causing adrenal fatigue like symptoms to me again?

It's weird, because my temperatures are getting up to 98.6, but I still feel extremely cold down to my core. Also, if I go outside for as little as 5 minutes in 50 degree temperature, I feel extremely cold for hours after coming back inside and my temp goes back down to 97.6 when I take my temp an hour later. It's like I can't adapt to anything even remotely cold. A few years ago, I could be very comfortable in shorts and t-shirt in temperature in the 50's.

Thanks for any help you may be able to give me.

### Dextery — 2013-05-27T21:46:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-preg-dhea-supplementation-and-sleep-cold-intolerance.7164/post-86960>

Your degree of coldness you feel and having a heart rate in the 40s and a high TSH indicate...as you suggest..hypothyroidism. The iodine may have perked up your thyroid the past three days to increase your heart rate.

The iodine supplementation may help you with your thyroid function.

But you need to get a thyroid panel done to include the following: Free T3, Free T4, reverse T3, & TSH. And you need to find a Doc that will understand the principles outlines in the following website:
Stopthethyroidmadness.com which will give you some guidelines as to what to look for. Docs that are open to the info there are few and far between as many have found out.

And you really need to read/re-read Brain Gut 12 [http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/](http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/)

Adrenal fatigue is a brain problem...not a adrenal gland problem.

And you really need to be drinking at least a gallon of non-fluoridated water a day.

### bennyha — 2013-05-28T02:18:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-preg-dhea-supplementation-and-sleep-cold-intolerance.7164/post-86981>

Thanks for your reply Dex. Yeah I have been following Epi-Paleo diet/Leptin Reset principles for over a year now. I have read and re-read all of the blogs many, many times. I don't currently do CT (but did in the past), and have even very low carb for a few years now. I developed a thyroid problem in the past 18 months, interesting enough when I started going low carb. I think Jack said in a previous post that if you aren't cold adapted (which I am defintely not) and you eat low carb, you run into problems.

I have gotten all those test you recommended above.

Free T4 (blood spot)...............1.3.......................0.7-2.5 ng/dL
Free T3 (blood spot)...............2.5.......................2.5-6.5 pg/ml
TSH (blood spot)....................3.5...................... .0.5-3.0 uU/ml
TPO (blood spot)....................25....................... .0-150 Iu/ml

Other labs I had done at Lab Corp in February. I don't have the exact results, but I did a full iron and ferritin lab and they all came back optimal. I did an HS-CRP, and it came back 0.4.
I also did another Free T3, and this time it came back lower and out of range low again, at 2.2.
I also took a Reverse T3 test, and although it came back in range, my free t3: rev t3 ratio (the one where its "suppose" to be above 20) came back at 11.7.

I don't have the funds nor the trust in a conventional Dr. I tried one in the past and it was a horrible waste of money. I started taking Armour once, but felt like shit and quit after a month. That was almost a year ago. I now know my RT3 problem was causing the T4 to turn into RT3, making me feel like shit and with a puffy face on Armour.

I feel like my adrenal fatigue problems has gotten better since those tests almost a year ago for cortisol. I worked with a holistic doctor for a few months and he said I was filled with metals and microorganisms. I worked over a 5 month period to rid of them, so I'm hoping that could bring down my RT3 as well.

I guess I'm basically wondering if I should try natural thyroid again and see if that helps me this time around.

Whats the prevailing thought around here on thyroid medication? Is it a long term need, or a temporary patch on my way to recovery that can help me warm up and start the hormone pathways?

### bennyha — 2013-05-28T02:20:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-preg-dhea-supplementation-and-sleep-cold-intolerance.7164/post-86982>

Also, I drink around 80 oz of spring water a day, and I think I urinate 20x or more a day, so I don't know how much good that it's even doing for me as I'm obviously peeing it right back out

### Huck — 2013-05-28T02:44:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-preg-dhea-supplementation-and-sleep-cold-intolerance.7164/post-86984>

The one thing that seems to help me the most is getting lots of sun. I started the epi-paleo diet about 7 months before good sun (above 50 degrees), and I think that was key. The sun never affected me this way before I went on epi-paleo.

First, make sure your vitamin d is not tanked. When mine was real low, nothing else I did seemed to matter. Once I got it up to 50-60 with supplementation, I found that I could spend hours in the sun and not burn, just tan. I spent 6 hours in the sun today doing hard work (digging post holes) and feel the best I have felt in weeks. Grounding and at least 2 hours a day in the sun seems to do it for me. Gardening is great because you ground when you pull weeds and get sun at the same time.

So you might try an experiment. Try getting at least 2 hours of sun a day for 30 or 60 days and then retest. Out of all the things I have tried, photons help me the most.

That is not pretty, I had it after Green Laser Prostate surgery. Can't get out of the house for a walk.

# Thread: Beginne; what is my problem
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beginne-what-is-my-problem.7165/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LRichard12 — 2013-05-27T17:16:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beginne-what-is-my-problem.7165/post-86945>

Hi my name is Lucas. I am 22 years old, 6foot tall, 150 lbs, around 12-14% body fat. I have recently (over the past year) lost 100 lbs (from 250 to 150). I was obese my entire life, suffered from high BP, chronic fatigue, laziness, low motivation, brain fog, poor memory, depression, etc. I then developed heart palpitations and the chroic fatigue and neuro problems worsened. I tried everything in the book, had every blood test, have taken every anti depressant and stimulant the neurologist and GP could throw at me. I've taken every supplement under the sun (and spent a small fortune doing so), and had even turned to drugs to help (found hydrocodon and tramadol worked the best). I've learned to manage and press on, by the grace of GOD, day by day drinking coffee, yerba mate, green tea, taking endless combinations of supplements, and eating what I thought was a balanced diet. Then after coming back from Africa (I am a missionary from the USA, based in Dublin, serving in West Africa) last year weighing in over 250lbs (probably closer to 300) and decided that it was time to lose weight. So I switched to a pure veggie, fruit, and low fat cottage cheese diet, cut out all added sugars and deserts, ate mueslie cereal, skim mile, flax seed, goji berries, bannnanas, and apple juice for breakfast, etc. I started jogging and hiking at least 3 mornings a week and eventually joined a gym. I lost a few lbs and felt a bit better though still fighting with chronic fatigue, low motivation, and heavily relient on coffee. Also was still experimenting with antidepressants.

Then I decided to go super low calorie, eating mostly protein shakes, low fat cottage cheese, and fat free unsweetened organic yogurt (used styevia to sweeten). I dropped a few more lbs, but was hungry all of the time! Then I was introduced to Atkins (thank GOD). I switched to Atkins and lost the rest of my wieght fairly quickly, going down below 150, while building muscle. I still stayed very low calorie (1200/day) for a while and ate only 3 meals a day, no snacks. However, every one kept telling me that I needed to up my calorie intake. So I uped them by adding in 2 snacks of nuts (either almonds or walnuts) and more olive, walnut, or coconut oil. Up until that point I had full appetite control, and had no trouble eating less than 1000 calories per day if I wanted to. Then once I added the snacks and uped my calories I began to get hungry. However I maintained control.

Then I went to Africa in February. While in Africa my appetite continued to increase, eventually becoming ravenous. I lost control for a couple of weeks and gorged on peanuts, gaining a couple of lbs. Then regained control forcing myself to stay around 1800 calories and <25 net carbs perday. Now I am back in Dublin and I'm over come by my hunger, my obsession with food, it is all I can think about, and I cant seem to stay full. I am eating 3 solid meals a day, under 20 net carbs, 100 grams protein, 150 grams of fat (mostly from olive oil and avacoados), and all of the low carb veggies that I can ingest. I still seem to be hungry.

Recently I discovered Mark Sission's Primal diet and the paleo diet, read MAstering Leptin, and the perfect 10 diet. I then came across Jack Kruses Leptin RX. After reading into it I think that my problem is low leptin. So I tried a primal refeed (sweet potatoes, berries, low fat organic unsweetened yogurt, honey, etc) last week, with around 500 grams carb, 50g fat, 130 or so protein, and nearly 3000 cals, which kind of helped. Then I started the Leptin RX , eating 50 grams of protein at breakfast (eggs, hard cheese, almonds, and coconut oil) after which I am still hungry. So today I am trying another refeed (roast beef (for morning protein) sweet potatoes, white potatoes, parsnips, low fat organic unsweetened yogurt (with stevia and FOS to sweeten)). I'm really hoping that this works! Tomorrow its back to low carb/Leptin Rx. I am trying to maintain nutritional ketosis (for the thereputic and weight loss benifits), however I find that first thing in the morning my fasting blood ketones are consistently .3 mmol (they where around 2.0 to 3.0 fasting before leaving for Africa in Feb), then creep up to nearly 2.0 by the afternoon. So I'm trying not to over do the protein (I keep to low 100's; 120 or so from eggs, hard natural cheese, nuts, and 2 meals of wild salmon a day), eat high fat (150 or so), and 20 net carbs (from veggies, organic unsweetened greek yogurt, and nuts). I have been abvoiding snacks and processed foods for over 1 month now, I drink only filtered water, go for everything organic, and take evey supplement recomemended by the lowcarb/paleo books I've read (I.E. GPC, PS, NAC, Acyl-L-Carnitine, L-Carnosine, vit d3, vit E, Magnesium, Potasium, Chromium, R-ALA, Calcium, Zinc, Omega 3, 6, 7, 9, extra DHA and EPA, vit C, green tea w/hoodia, cinnamon, kelp (also eat Kombu), 5 grams+ natural sea salt, Melatonin (2mg 1 hour before bed w/ magnesium to sleep), broad spectrum probiotics, and 40 grams of F.O.S per day, and an iron free multi vit). Still have little to no energy, irritable some times, weak, hungry!, obsessed with food, and craving sweet potatos and chocolate. It wasn't like this before. What's my problem and what do you recommend I do to fix it.

BTW I just ordered PQQ and reservatrol, will add it to my L-Carnosine, NAC, L Carnitine, RALA, GPC, and PS; hoping that that will help.

Please help. Thank you!

Lucas

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-27T17:45:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beginne-what-is-my-problem.7165/post-86948>

you need more fat with your 50 gms of protein in your diet......when you lose this much weight fast it is critical. I have not talked about this but this is exactly what happened to me when I lost 133 lbs in 11 months.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-27T18:04:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/now-q10-has-a-powered-study.7166/post-86951>

Professor Mortensen said: "CoQ10 is the first medication to improve survival in chronic heart failure since ACE inhibitors and beta blockers more than a decade ago and should be added to standard heart failure therapy."

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-27T18:04:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/now-q10-has-a-powered-study.7166/post-86952>

References: 1. Heart Failure Congress 2013 [http://www.escardio.org/congresses/hf2013/Pages/welcome.aspx?hit=nav](http://www.escardio.org/congresses/hf2013/Pages/welcome.aspx?hit=nav) 2. SA Mortensen, A Kumar, P Dolliner, et al. The effect of coenzyme Q10 on morbidity and mortality in chronic heart failure. Results from the Q-SYMBIO study. Presented at Heart Failure Congress 2013 Final Programme Number 440. The full title of the Q-SYMBIO study is: "Coenzyme Q10 as adjunctive treatment of chronic heart failure: a randomised double blind multicentre trial with focus on changes in symptoms, biomarker status with BNP and long term outcome"

This thread comes quite in handy... today my dad visited his cardiologist. I explained to him many times how statins are not the answer to fight CVD, that cholesterol is life and that needs to be managed through lifestyle... and to tell his doc to reconsider the therapy... he asked her but she made no sense, she argued that statins not only work by lowering LDL but that their have other benefits that he needs. Yeah I'm aware that they are anti-inflammatory and reduce interferons but that can certainly be ruled out with proper diet and lifestyle, then you have good immune function plus the benefits of having natural LDL levels to fight diseases... but of course she's not dialed in this idea. Higher LDL than 140 is bad, period...

The saddest thing is that my dad believes in me and I think he knows that I'm into the correct path, yet he is very troubled to fight and go agains his doc's RX... he would be medically left alone since he doesn't have a private insurance like me to go find a better one... Today I talked to him and explained how that strategy will lead to poor performance mitochondria, like cars, that will downgrade the ferrari in us into a thrased car... this idea has inspired a [quick post on my blog](http://www.nutricioblog.com/2014/05/08/sobre-els-mitocondris-el-coenzim-q10-leficiencia-i-els-cotxes/). I've Ubiquinol at home for myself, I love to give advice but don't like to interfere with other people's decisions even if they're my parents but I think this time I'll push a little bit to get him starting using it.

### Gracelind — 2014-05-09T06:40:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/now-q10-has-a-powered-study.7166/post-125982>

Hi Albert,
Is your dad taking CoQ10 with the statin? Research shows that statins interfere with CoQ10 and that supplementing may help ward off the muscle pains and sometimes myopathy that some patients develop. Because of the muscle disease I have (myositis/myopathy) I know quite a few people online who developed pretty devasting polymyositis or dermatomyositis after taking statins. For some years the docs refused to acknowledge a connection until the evidence was overwhelming. The percentage of folks who take statins then develop myositis is relatively small for those people it's a major issue.

If his doc hasn't put him on CoQ10 she's quite behind on that part of the research.

My father was on lovastatin for years. Due to my mother's illness turning both their lives upside down we finally realized that the statin was affecting his brain and his thinking. He complained of being so confused all the time. When, with the doc's okay, we took him off, he stopped complaining about his thinking, though he still showed signs of mild dementia (he was 87). After a mild MI a different doc put him on simvastatin and the confusion flared again till he discontinued it.

It's so hard watch those we love go down the wrong path, thinking they are doing the right thing.

### Lahelada — 2014-05-09T13:54:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/now-q10-has-a-powered-study.7166/post-126014>

I am sure that you can find a doc that will respond if you reason awith them as you do here.A lot of docs I think work from the principle "If all you got is a hammer ,eveeything looks like a nail" Bring them a new tool!

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-09T14:25:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/now-q10-has-a-powered-study.7166/post-126015>

@[Gracelind](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/329/) I'm aware of that link, thank you very much for you insight!! Yesterday I told him to start with the Ubiquinol I have at home and he agreed, right now is a good move for him to do but @[Lahelada](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6531/) is right, we should go find the right doctor to suit a better strategy, problem is that after all the things he went through and now he seems like he has beat them and feel 'overrall well', his perception of reality is that probably he can keep like this and make it to elderly hopefully without impact... well, I think that's what he wants to believe. I'm not like that, I try to analyse what the probable outcomes will be on the future based on the knowledge I can play with right now, and I see a different picture for him not based on my wishes.

PD: You're right, knowing what many don't know and being not able to apply it to the ones you love because the force that leads the mass is stronger than any effort you might do, it's so upsetting... I guess there's some times when even if you know better you are not allowed to do better.

# Thread: Surgery help!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-help.7169/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### juliebgood — 2013-05-27T21:49:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-help.7169/post-86961>

Hi, folks! I'm a long-time off-and-on lurker, occasional commenter, so you probably wouldn't know me, but I'm hoping people will help me out regardless, as this is the best place I can think of to come for help. As an absolute last resort, I'm having my first major surgery in a month, breast reduction surgery, and I'm really freaked out about taking antibiotics and healing well after surgery for optimal results, especially the healing well part.

If anybody has suggestions for things I should be doing pre and post-surgery to minimize the damage from antibiotics, prevent scarring, and generally heal well, I'd be eternally grateful.

Any help would be appreciated - thanks in advance.

PS: The thing is, if somebody were asking \*me\* this question, I bet I'd be able to rattle off a few obvious suggestions, but when you yourself are in a critical situation, the emotional turmoil can short-circuit your ability to think  And besides, the internet is way cleverer than I, so I'd be foolish not to ask!

### Dextery — 2013-05-27T21:59:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-help.7169/post-86962>

First be sure your serum Vit D3 level is pushing 100ng/ml For elective surgery, Dr. K always pumps up his surgery patients prior to the table. You can take 50000IU daily til surgery and this will pump up your immune system so that your need for antibiotics will be minimal.

Second, Get cold packs, ice packs and ice your breasts as much as you can possibly do for as long as you can do prior to surgery. Hopefully you will have a month to do this prior. The outcomes of surgery are always better...short recovery time in hospital and much quicker healing. Dr K always has his patients ice the area the incisions are to be made prior to surgery.

And do as much cold thermogenesis on your entire body every day as much as you can prior to surgery to prepare your body for the insult that you are undergoing.

### juliebgood — 2013-05-27T22:11:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-help.7169/post-86965>

Thanks, Dextery! I'm supplementing D3 now and soaking up the sun, also CT-ing a while every other day or so, but it sounds like I should really ramp up both.

Do you know what it is about spot CT-ing the incision area that makes it heal better? I can understand doing that \*after\* surgery to aid healing, but why you would do it for days and weeks beforehand confuses me.

### Dextery — 2013-05-27T22:26:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-help.7169/post-86966>

Nope. Can't tell you why pre surgery spot CT works. I only know that JK does it with great outcomes for his patients. And yes, do ramp up the CT daily...perhaps 2x daily and get your D3 level up NOW.

Post surgery CT is also fabulous also for healing. You will amaze your surgeon how well you do post op.

Here is the answer to pre-op spot CT. [http://www.jackkruse.com/peri-operative-optimal-surgical-considerations/](http://www.jackkruse.com/peri-operative-optimal-surgical-considerations/)

### Adria — 2013-05-28T22:46:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-help.7169/post-87026>

Use ice every day before, concentrating where the surgeon will cut. Do this as many times per day as possible. Eat strict epi-paleo. Get sunlight and ground yourself whenever possible. When you wake in the recovery ask for ice bag. I had brain surgery and they didn't even have an ice bag available in ICU but the nurse made one makeshift. Keep icing the incisions every day afterwards. After the wounds were completely closed I used coconut oil on the incision to help minimize scars.

Take care and hope all goes wonderful for you! I have a friend who had the breast reduction surgery and she is extremely happy with the results.

### juliebgood — 2013-05-29T03:52:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-help.7169/post-87050>

Dextry, you are a god. Thank you so much for that link. I had no idea Jack had done such a post; he's been so prolific it's hard keeping up with the man! That post should be invaluable. Thanks so much again!

### juliebgood — 2013-05-29T03:55:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-help.7169/post-87051>

Thanks so much, Adria! They all sound like great suggestions. I really need to start with the ice packs, and also generally ramp up the rest of what I'm doing. That's pretty neat that you were spot CT-ing after brain surgery!

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-05-27T23:25:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-fundamental-blog-for-understanding-is-now-live-qb-8-quantum-scaling.7170/post-86971>

Enjoyed this post thoroughly, going to give it some time then re-read it and annotate it to really let it sink in. It reinforces a lot of what I find to be subjectively true, and it reinforces your whole idea about the "quilt". I spoke a lot about 'awareness' in my philosophy class, and this blog really resonates with that idea. I'd love to sit down and have a chat with you about things outside of wellness, nutrition, disease, etc. and more geared toward the dualistic nature of life and reality. That being said, I have some questions and comments about what has been said in this blog.

I see how your tying things up in terms of 'hard work', 'dedication', and 'density'. I'm making metaphorical bridges to how that correlates with my life and well-being, or anyone in general on the road to recover/optimal. I think you're one of few, probably only a couple people who look at health in terms of a unified system and not fragmented units, and I love that. Using BHRT can obviously help people by increasing hormone levels, but BHRT needs to be looked at in the grand scale of things..... we can give you this and it will increase your testosterone levels, BUT if we use this improperly it may destroy other hormones due to your clock being off (ultradian/circadian rhythms) and we may damage your endogenous production because we didn't take into account all the variables outside of one simple number on a piece of paper. I could see this in terms of diet as well. You provide yourself with the density (nutrient/electron density), maybe not epi-paleo but a step in the right direction (you eliminated bad fats and added grass-fed butter to your diet), but the density doesn't make up for the directional flow of things - although you are eating 'healthier' you didn't look at the macro aspect of life... your lifestyle choices, you still are pessimistic which causes unneeded stress which stresses (not in a hormetic way) every organ and gland in your body.... and then you slip up on your new diet. I see this in the ketogenic performance aspect as well. Many people are just simply stuck in the dogma and not willing to be open minded. Let's just say hypothetically everything we learned here is false, how do we know until we try? Yet people will tell you that you need carbs for performance/strength/explosiveness/muscle/thyroid/etc., but get the hell out of some disunified aspect of the literature and look at it connected to everything, then try it for yourself. We know fat is more dense than carbs in terms of ATP.... the only thing lacking is the discipline (to adapt, to put up with the adaptation phase with decreased performance, and to change) and the work (going out there and kicking ass regardless of what people say).

My only question was how you are going to tie this into fueling performance when on the podcast today/night but I get the feeling I see where you're headed.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-fundamental-blog-for-understanding-is-now-live-qb-8-quantum-scaling.7170/post-86980>

Here is more for the disbelievers like Peter Attia, Robb Wolf, et al about ketogenic diets and optimal performance. The quantum world works on stochastic calculus......not ordinary linear calculus. Stochastic calculus inherently assumes a square root dependence on the variable of time. This is why markets are random and why people results are random. What this implies is that when time scales are short the result will be quite random. As time lengthens then the real benefits show up. It is why some people have massive improvements in a short period of time when they get all the details correct.......and why other s never do. It also belies why ketogenic performance takes 24-36 months to reveal itself. Too bad the biochemists don't read more physics. They might learn a few things.

Estrogen receptor b and the progression of prostate cancer:
role of
5a-androstane-3b,17b-diol

Endocrine-Related Cancer (2010) 17 731–742
\------------------------------------
(3b-Adiol) = 5a-androstane-3b,17b-diol
\------------------------------------
.......................

These data as a whole indicate
that testosterone might control prostate physiology
through several contrasting mechanisms:
a) androgenic signals via AR/testosterone and AR/DHT complexes,
b) estrogenic signals via ER (a and b)/E2
and
c) alternative estrogenic signaling via ER (b)/3b-Adiol.
The goal of this study was to assess the effects of 3b-Adiol on PC cell proliferation, migration, and
invasiveness in vitro as well as in vivo in xenograft models of tumorigenesis in nude mice

**3b-Adiol**, DHT, and E2(R,R0)-cis-diethyltetrahydro-2,8-chrysenediol (R,R0-THC) were obtained from **Sigma–Aldrich**.

obtained from Supelco, Milan, Italy. All solvents and reagents were HPLC grade (Sigma–Aldrich). For the experiments in vivo ,3b-Adiol was dissolved in sesame oil, and injected s.c. at a dose of 2.5 mg/kg. Control animals were injected with the same amount of vehicle.

bottom left page 372
[http://erc.endocrinology-journals.org/content/17/3/731.full.pdf](http://erc.endocrinology-journals.org/content/17/3/731.full.pdf)

\------------------

makes
3b-Adiol

..

[http://www.sigmaaldrich.com](http://www.sigmaaldrich.com)

[http://www.sigmaaldrich.com/sigma-a...ldrich&cm\_guid=4-\_-6559831479-\_-858555629-\_-e](http://www.sigmaaldrich.com/sigma-aldrich/home.html?cm_remove=&cm_mmc=Microsoft-_-SIAL+Branded-_-e-_-sigmaaldrich&cm_guid=4-_-6559831479-_-858555629-_-e)

.......

[http://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog...53286&mode=match partialmax&lang=pl&region=PL](http://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog/PageNotFound.jsp?dym=3b%20ediol&interface=All&term=3b-Adiol&lang=pl&region=PL&focus=product&N=0+220003048+219853218+219853286&mode=match%20partialmax&lang=pl&region=PL)

3b-Adiol

Atherosclerosis 2010-09-01
The androgen derivative
5@a-androstane-3@b,17@b-diol
inhibits tumor necrosis factor @a and lipopolysaccharide induced inflammatory response in human endothelial cells and in mice aorta

> Abstract
> Background: An increasing body of evidence suggests that testosterone may exert beneficial effects against the development of atherosclerosis. These effects are thought to be the consequence of its conversion into estradiol and the activation of the estrogen receptors; however a direct role of androgens, such as dihydrotestosterone, has also been proposed. More recently, it has been shown that the transformation of the dihydrotestosterone to 5@a-androstane-3@a,17@b-diol (3@a-diol) and 5@a-androstane-3@b,17@b-diol (3@b-Adiol), generates two molecules unable to bind the androgen receptor, but with a high affinity for the estrogen receptors (ERs) in particular the @b isoform. As the actions of testosterone may result from the balance between androgenic and estrogenic molecules originating from its catabolism, we investigated the effects of the 3@b-Adiol on inflammatory responses in vitro in human endothelial cells and ex vivo in mice aortas. Methods and results: 3@b-Adiol reverts the pro-inflammatory gene expression pattern induced by TNF-@a in HUVECs as determined by a cDNA microrray approach. Q-real-time PCR and protein array approaches confirmed that TNF-@a-induced ICAM-1, VCAM-1 and ELAM-1 as well as MCP-1 and IL-6 induction was affected upon 3@b-Adiol pre-incubation. ICI 182780, an estrogen receptor antagonist and R,R-THC, an estrogen receptor @b antagonist, counteracted the effect of 3@b-Adiol while bicalutamide, an androgen receptor antagonist, had minor effects. 3@b-Adiol exerted a similar action on macrophages. Finally in castrated male mice, 3@b-Adiol significantly counteracted the LPS mediated mRNA induction of IL-6, ELAM-1and PECAM-1 in the aortas. Conclusion: 3@b-Adiol reverts in vitro the TNF-@a and LPS induced pro-inflammatory activation of endothelial cells and macrophages. 3@b-Adiol in vivo modulates the inflammatory response induced by LPS in the arterial vascular wall.

page 733 left middle

> In orthotopic xenograft experiments, mice were daily injected s.c. with 2.5 mg/kg 3b-Adiol in sesame oil or with corresponding amount of vehicle (sesame oil).
> Silastic pellets (Degania Silicone Ltd, Degania Bet, Israel) were prepared by cutting a silastic pipe (diameter 1.5 mm) into small segments of 1 cm long.
> These segments were filled with 3b-Adiol powder (or left empty in vehicle-treated mice), and closed to the edge with a double knot made by a surgical silk thread.
> Silastic pellets were then implanted s.c. on the left side of the mouse just behind the anterior leg using a trochar.

> Results 3b-Adiol regulation of PC cell growth In the initial set of experiments, we have measured the effects of increasing concentration of 3b-Adiol (0.1–10 nM) on AR-negative PC cell proliferation.
> In growth course experiments, 3b-Adiol significantly reduced (about 50% reduction at 10 nM, the maximal concentration tested), after 48-h treatment, the number of DU145, PC3, and PC3-Luc cells (Fig. 1A–C);
> the cell number reduction was due to inhibition of the proliferative rate of the cells as confirmed by thymidine

page 739 right bottom

> **It has also to be underlined that, on the basis of our results, the classical androgen depletion therapy widely utilized to treat PC should be re-evaluated.** The complete removal of androgens, which can be obtained using GnRH analog treatments, would also remove 3b-Adiol, the agent that exert a protection against PC cell proliferation and metastasis formation. It must be noted that the classical side effects of these drugs are generally related to estrogen deficiency that occurs as a result of treatment. These are often considered the results of E2 deprivation, but the estrogenic effects of 3b-Adiol might contribute to these systemic alterations (Freedland et al. 2009). Therefore, the protection of 3b-Adiol will be preserved using AR antagonists only.
> A retrospective analysis should thus be conducted to determine whether the androgen-insensitive PC may
> become more aggressive because of the protection offered by 3b-Adiol is eliminated by the GnRH analog-based therapy

/

### JanSz — 2013-05-28T02:47:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-cancer-how-to-treat.7171/post-86986>

[http://meridianvalleylab.com/1679-2](http://meridianvalleylab.com/1679-2)

The Anticancer Testosterone Metabolite 3β-Adiol

Published in: Townsend Letter

By. Dr. Jonathan v. Wright, MD

Age-related decline of testosterone in men is often seen as part of the “normal” aging process. Why, then, does the risk of prostate cancer increase with age? We could blame it on declining hormones, but not all men with declining (or elevated, for that matter) hormones get prostate cancer. For a long time, male hormone testing has been centered on testing only testosterone and sometimes (more frequently lately) free testosterone. One heavily researched area is the effect of dihydrotestosterone (DHT) on the prostate. But is testing DHT enough? The short answer: No.

Much of the DHT research has been associated with the patent medicine finasteride (Proscar, Propecia) and other related patent medicines. Finasteride greatly slows the metabolism of testosterone into the more potent (and more carcinogenic) metabolite DHT. (For the technically inclined, finasteride is a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor.)

Since DHT is generally agreed to be carcinogenic, the thought was that reducing the transformation of testosterone into DHT would reduce cancer risk. In the Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial (PCPT), 18,882 men over age 55 years with normal prostate examinations and a PSA below 3.0 were randomly assigned finasteride or placebo for seven years.1

Slightly Lower Cancer Risk, Much Higher Killer Cancer Risk
The results were a surprise to the researchers, who found – as they expected – a lower rate of cancer in the finasteride group than in the placebo group (18.4% versus 24.4%). What surprised them was the considerably higher number of significantly aggressive cancers – for the technically inclined, higher Gleason scores – among those in the finasteride group with cancer versus those with cancer in the placebo group (37% versus 22.3%).

That’s why you never saw a television commercial about finasteride preventing prostate cancer. To be accurate it would have to say, “Take finasteride! It lowers your risk of prostate cancer, but if you do get prostate cancer, you’re more likely to die of it!”

The PCPT researchers concluded, “finasteride prevents or delays the appearance of prostate cancer, but this possible benefit and a reduced risk of urinary problems must be weighed against sexual side effects and the increased risk of high-grade prostate cancer.”

A separate meta-analysis published as a Cochrane Review found that 5a-reductase inhibitors, including finasteride, have inadequate evidence to say that these patent medicines reduce mortality, in terms of prostate cancer.2

It’s true that if DHT alone is considered, elevated levels of DHT might be thought to increase your prostate cancer risk, as DHT is a procarcinogenic (for the technically inclined, dedifferentiating) meta\*bolite.3,4

But Nature hasn’t provided DHT alone! If we take the metabolites of DHT into consideration, too, elevated DHT may or may not have this effect. One of these metabolites may actually offset or even reduce any DHT-increased risk. How does that happen?

Stop Suppressing Your Natural Defense
After testosterone is converted to DHT, DHT is in turn normally metabolized into a relatively smaller quantity of 5a-androstane-3a,17b-diol (abbreviated as 3a-Adiol), and a usually larger amount of 5a-androstane-3b,17b-diol (abbreviated as 3b-Adiol). These same researchers also report that while nearly all the 3a-Adiol is converted back to DHT (which presumably makes 3a-Adiol a “pre-procarcinogen”), the 3b-Adiol does not convert back to 5a-DHT. Very importantly, they report that 3b-Adiol is an anticarcinogen (for the technically inclined, a redifferentiating agent) that activates estrogen receptor beta, an anticarcinogenic estrogen receptor present in large numbers in the prostate gland.5 (Estrogen receptor beta is present in many other tissues in both sexes, but that’s a topic to be explored at another time.)

What’s that? A testosterone metabolite that activates an estrogen receptor? A testosterone metabolite that doesn’t activate any androgen receptors at all? That’s right, and (very unfortunately for the men involved) my observation has been that many men who take finasteride to suppress procarcinogenic DHT end up suppressing their own anticarcinogenic 3b-Adiol even more, thus shifting the procarcinogen (DHT) to anticarcinogen (3b-Adiol) ratio toward a greater risk of cancer. I’ve also seen this occasionally with high doses of saw palmetto.

In a letter to the editor of the New England Journal of Medicine, Otabek Imamov, MD, et al. state, “\[DHT\] is the fulcrum in this balance. It suggests that finasteride, by blocking the conversion of testosterone to \[DHT\], inhibits the production of \[3b-Adiol\] thus suppressing \[the anticarcinogenic activity of\] ERb and preventing the \[re\]-differentiation of epithelium. This mechanism could account for the higher incidence of poorly differentiated tumors in the finasteride group in the Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial.”6

A review in the Biology of Reproduction Journal states, “We believe that a higher incidence of low-differentiated \[more aggressive\] tumors in the finasteride-treated arm observed in the PCPT is caused by altering the normal differentiation of prostatic epithelium in the environment lacking the natural ERb ligand – \[3b-Adiol\].”7

Research has found some very specific things that 3b-Adiol does to inhibit prostate cancer growth. According to the title of a 2005 research report: “The androgen derivative 5a-androstane-3b, 17b-diol \[3b-Adiol\] inhibits prostate cancer cell migration through activation of the estrogen receptor beta subtype.”8 Other researchers reported that “3b-Adiol not only inhibits PC3-Luc cell \[a specific type of prostate cancer cell\] migratory properties, but also induces a broader antitumor phenotype \[type of cell\] by decreasing the proliferation \[growth\] rate, increasing cell adhesion \[cancer cells don't "stick" as normal cells do\] and reducing invasive capabilities in vitro.”9 But these researchers went beyond test-tubes to living mice, writing “In vivo, continuous administration of 3b-Adiol reduces growth of established tumors and counteracts metastasis formation when PC3-Luc cells are engrafted subcutaneously in nude mice or are injected into the prostate.”

The conclusion to this research article was very encouraging: “Since 3b-Adiol has no androgenic activity, and cannot be converted to androgenic compounds, the effects here described entail a novel potential application of this agent against human PC.”9 A novel potential application of 3b-Adiol, a totally natural human testosterone, against human prostate cancer! Where are the headlines? This article was published in 2010!

For the really technically inclined, here are several “mechanisms of action” of 3b-Adiol, all of which come from stimulation of estrogen receptor beta:

Â§ repression of VEGF-A (vascular endothelial growth factor A) expression
Â§ destabilization of HIF-1a (hypoxia-inducible factor 1a)
Â§ reduction of “Snail1″ (can’t identify this acronym) relocation from the cytoplasm to the nucleus of cancer cells

According to the researchers who published the above mechanisms of action, “… high Gleason grade cancers … exhibit significantly more HIF-1a and VEGF-A and Snail1 nuclear localization compared to low Gleason grade cancers.”10

cont next post

### JanSz — 2013-05-28T02:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-cancer-how-to-treat.7171/post-86987>

continuation from post #3

Procarcinogenic and Anticarcinogenic Metabolites
The testosterone Â® DHT Â® 3b-Adiol metabolic pathway is very similar in its pro- and anticancer effects to the estrogen metabolic pathway estrone (E1) Â® 16a-hydroxyestrone (16a-OHE1) Â® estriol (E3). While 16a-OHE1 has a “good side” and helps protect against osteoporosis, its bad side is that too much 16-OHE1 increases breast and prostate cancer risk.11-14 But like DHT, which also increases cancer risk, 16a-OHE1 is transformed into the anticarcinogenic “good-guy” (more precisely, “good-girl”) estriol. Just as we want to promote the formation of more estriol to “tilt” the balance of a woman’s estrogens in an anticarcinogenic direction, we want to make sure that 3b-Adiol is more abundant than DHT, to “tilt” the balance of a man’s androgens in an anticarcinogenic direction.

Until the recent research about 3b-Adiol became available, when physicians saw an elevated DHT test, they frequently recommended reducing the man’s testosterone dose – even if he didn’t feel as well with that lower dose – because of understandable concern about higher cancer risk. There has been no test for 3b-Adiol, no way for men using testosterone replacement or the physicians prescribing it to assess the pro- and anticarcinogenic balance of major testosterone metabolites.

The Test That Could Save Your Life
Now that test is available. Thanks to the work of Dawn Huo, PhD, at Meridian Valley Laboratory, you or your physician can request a blood test for testosterone, DHT, 3a-Adiol, and 3b-Adiol (full name: 5a-androstane-3b,17b-diol), check the relative proportions of each, and have a more clear idea about your cancer risk. Termed “testosterone metabolites,” this blood serum test also measures androstenedione (spelled almost but not quite exactly the same way as “androstanediol”), another androgen metabolite, and 3a-Adiol (full name: 5a-androstane-3a,17b-diol. (Extensive quality control testing by Dr. Huo has shown that presently available tests for urine DHT are far less accurate than serum DHT.)

Remember, both 3b-Adiol and 3a-Adiol are metabolites of DHT, but researchers have found that almost all 3a-Adiol turns back into DHT (thus making it a procarcinogenic precursor), while none of the 3b-Adiol – which stimulates estrogen receptor beta (ERb), and is entirely anticarcinogenic – can “back-metabolize” into DHT again.6

Theoretically, if a man’s 3b-Adiol level is greater than the sum of his DHT plus 3a-Adiol (3b-Adiol > DHT + 3a-Adiol), his “testosterone metabolite ratio” should be anticarcinogenic. Please keep in mind that at this time, the testosterone metabolite ratio is theoretical, supported by inference from considerable scientific research, but not absolutely proven. Unlike the 2-hydroxyestrogen/16a- hydroxyestrogen ratio (“2/16″ ratio), which has been proved to predict lower and higher cancer risk in premenopausal women, or the estrogen quotient (EQ), the testosterone metabolite ratio has not yet been studied in groups of men over time, which is necessary to absolutely prove a theory.

Over the years, I’ve had occasion to observe the multiple adverse effects of patent medications on normal metabolism. My observation has been that when men are given a patent medication that slows down the metabolization of testosterone into DHT, much more often than not, 3b-Adiol is suppressed even further, so the testosterone metabolite ratio is rebalanced in a procarcinogenic direction. As Professor Imamov explained, this is very likely why the Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial found a significantly higher proportion of highly aggressive cancers in the men who despite taking the patent medicine finasteride still developed prostate cancer.7 Note that, in addition to finasteride (Proscar, Propecia), dutasteride (Avodart) can have this effect, as can very large quantities of saw palmetto. And as “bonus points,” many men who take these patent medicines also have diminished libido, and varying degrees of erectile dysfunction (ED).

By contrast, I usually recommended quantities of zinc, GLA (gamma-linolenic acid), and other natural inhibitors of the testosterone Â® DHT transformation are “gentler”; their use rarely results in a procarcinogenic testosterone metabolite ratio.

Three Reasons to Test for 3b-Adiol and the Procarcinogenic Estrogen Metabolites.
I realize that despite my best attempts to explain testosterone metabolite testing in plain English, the preceding is highly technical. Please consider testosterone metabolite testing if you’re:

1\. using testosterone replacement therapy. For unknown reasons, “replacement” testosterone metabolizes into DHT more than internally secreted testosterone. It’s best to make sure that if this happens, you have a greater quantity of 3b-Adiol than DHT + 3a-Adiol;

2\. taking Proscar, Propecia, Avodart, or very large amounts – usually over 500 milligrams – of saw palmetto daily. Particularly with the use of these patent medications, the “testosterone metabolite ratio” is most often decidedly procarcinogenic;

3\. a man whose family has a history of prostate cancer. In this case, you want to check for estrogen metabolites too, as a low “2/16″ estrogen ratio has been associated with higher prostate cancer risk.12

The testosterone metabolite test can be drawn at any lab and sent from there by the usual means to the Meridian Valley Laboratory ([www.meridianvalleylab.com](http://www.meridianvalleylab.com) or 425-271-8689 ) for performance and reporting

Potential Endogenous Stimulation of 3b-Adiol
For several months in 2011, compounding pharmacies were able to supply “3b-Adiol” at a relatively reasonable prices. But – according to compounding pharmacists – a certain agency of los federales began to interfere with freedom of trade. The price quickly escalated, and then 3b-Adiol became entirely unavailable. Surprised, anyone?

Fortunately, researchers are reporting possibilities for stimulating the natural endogenous biosynthesis of 3b-Adiol with natural substances which stimulate 3b-HSD and/or 17b-HSD, the enzymes that convert 5a-DHT into 3b-Adiol. But there’s a caution: Although these studies are theoretically promising, none of them have as yet actually measured “before and after” quantities of 3b-Adiol itself, but rather activity and/or quantity of the enzymes that “lead to” 3b-Adiol. Until this research has been done, the best alternative is measuring the “before and after” levels in individuals, especially individuals found to personally have low 3b-Adiol levels. (In my own practice, I’ve observed that several of the items below have been associated with improved 3b-Adiol levels in individuals, but it’s too early to report that any one is reliably associated with improvement in low 3b-Adiol levels.)

Let’s start with (no kidding) coconut oil and olive oil. In 2008 and 2009, researchers reported that these two oils, used separately, significantly stimulated the activity of 3b-HSD and 17b-HSD, as well as significantly raising testosterone levels in experimental animals, while grapeseed oil and soy oil did not have any significant effect.15,16

Then there’s our old “male health” standby, zinc. Studies in male rats demonstrated that zinc deficiency decreased 3b-HSD activity. Zinc deficiency also was associated with a very significant reduction in testosterone itself.17

Hypothyroidism and “subclinical” hypothyroidism are relatively common. Here’s another reason to be ever-vigilant for these problems: T3 stimulates 3b-HSD type 2.18 Although this might suggest that T3 may stimulate 3b-Adiol, there are as yet no publications exploring this possibility.

However, it’s easy enough to check in any one individual with lower than desirable 3b-Adiol.

3b-HSD also requires NAD (niacinamide adenine dinucleotide), but once again there are no studies yet linking NADH supplementation with improved levels of 3b-Adiol.19
In a study of adrenal cell activity, lithium was reported to increase synthesis of 3b-HSD type 2. (Obviously the adrenals and testes are entirely different, but the 3b-HSD enzyme is the same enzyme in both areas).

Lastly (for now) all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) has been shown to increase 3b-HSD type 2.20 As too much ATRA can become toxic, this one is available only by prescription.

\======================================

[http://www.questdiagnostics.com/testcenter/BUOrderInfo.action?tc=16808P&labCode=SJC](http://www.questdiagnostics.com/testcenter/BUOrderInfo.action?tc=16808P&labCode=SJC)

**
3a-Androstanediol Glucuronide (3-Alpha Diol G) (5276X)

Androstanediol Glucuronide (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500881 CPT Code: 82154

[http://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog...53286&mode=match partialmax&lang=pl&region=PL](http://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog/PageNotFound.jsp?dym=3b%20ediol&interface=All&term=3b-Adiol&lang=pl&region=PL&focus=product&N=0+220003048+219853218+219853286&mode=match%20partialmax&lang=pl&region=PL)

3b-Adiol

Atherosclerosis 2010-09-01
The androgen derivative
5@a-androstane-3@b,17@b-diol
inhibits tumor necrosis factor @a and lipopolysaccharide induced inflammatory response in human endothelial cells and in mice aorta

> Abstract
> Background: An increasing body of evidence suggests that testosterone may exert beneficial effects against the development of atherosclerosis. These effects are thought to be the consequence of its conversion into estradiol and the activation of the estrogen receptors; however a direct role of androgens, such as dihydrotestosterone, has also been proposed. More recently, it has been shown that the transformation of the dihydrotestosterone to 5@a-androstane-3@a,17@b-diol (3@a-diol) and 5@a-androstane-3@b,17@b-diol (3@b-Adiol), generates two molecules unable to bind the androgen receptor, but with a high affinity for the estrogen receptors (ERs) in particular the @b isoform. As the actions of testosterone may result from the balance between androgenic and estrogenic molecules originating from its catabolism, we investigated the effects of the 3@b-Adiol on inflammatory responses in vitro in human endothelial cells and ex vivo in mice aortas. Methods and results: 3@b-Adiol reverts the pro-inflammatory gene expression pattern induced by TNF-@a in HUVECs as determined by a cDNA microrray approach. Q-real-time PCR and protein array approaches confirmed that TNF-@a-induced ICAM-1, VCAM-1 and ELAM-1 as well as MCP-1 and IL-6 induction was affected upon 3@b-Adiol pre-incubation. ICI 182780, an estrogen receptor antagonist and R,R-THC, an estrogen receptor @b antagonist, counteracted the effect of 3@b-Adiol while bicalutamide, an androgen receptor antagonist, had minor effects. 3@b-Adiol exerted a similar action on macrophages. Finally in castrated male mice, 3@b-Adiol significantly counteracted the LPS mediated mRNA induction of IL-6, ELAM-1and PECAM-1 in the aortas. Conclusion: 3@b-Adiol reverts in vitro the TNF-@a and LPS induced pro-inflammatory activation of endothelial cells and macrophages. 3@b-Adiol in vivo modulates the inflammatory response induced by LPS in the arterial vascular wall.

page 733 left middle

> In orthotopic xenograft experiments, mice were daily injected s.c. with 2.5 mg/kg 3b-Adiol in sesame oil or with corresponding amount of vehicle (sesame oil).
> Silastic pellets (Degania Silicone Ltd, Degania Bet, Israel) were prepared by cutting a silastic pipe (diameter 1.5 mm) into small segments of 1 cm long.
> These segments were filled with 3b-Adiol powder (or left empty in vehicle-treated mice), and closed to the edge with a double knot made by a surgical silk thread.
> Silastic pellets were then implanted s.c. on the left side of the mouse just behind the anterior leg using a trochar.

> Results 3b-Adiol regulation of PC cell growth In the initial set of experiments, we have measured the effects of increasing concentration of 3b-Adiol (0.1–10 nM) on AR-negative PC cell proliferation.
> In growth course experiments, 3b-Adiol significantly reduced (about 50% reduction at 10 nM, the maximal concentration tested), after 48-h treatment, the number of DU145, PC3, and PC3-Luc cells (Fig. 1A–C);
> the cell number reduction was due to inhibition of the proliferative rate of the cells as confirmed by thymidine

page 739 right bittom

> **It has also to be underlined that, on the basis of our results, the classical androgen depletion therapy widely utilized to treat PC should be re-evaluated.** The complete removal of androgens, which can be obtained using GnRH analog treatments, would also remove 3b-Adiol, the agent that exert a protection against PC cell proliferation and metastasis formation. It must be noted that the classical side effects of these drugs are generally related to estrogen deficiency that occurs as a result of treatment. These are often considered the results of E2 deprivation, but the estrogenic effects of 3b-Adiol might contribute to these systemic alterations (Freedland et al. 2009). Therefore, the protection of 3b-Adiol will be preserved using AR antagonists only.
> A retrospective analysis should thus be conducted to determine whether the androgen-insensitive PC may
> become more aggressive because of the protection offered by 3b-Adiol is eliminated by the GnRH analog-based therapy

# Thread: Still Not Sure If I'm Leptin Resistant
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-not-sure-if-im-leptin-resistant.7172/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-05-28T06:00:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-not-sure-if-im-leptin-resistant.7172/post-86999>

**MODS PLEASE DELETE - POSTED IN THE WRONG AREA**

Was just reading Jack's epic blog post on leptin resistance:
[http://www.jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/](http://www.jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/)

Just reading the section on determining if you're leptin resistant where he says:

> How do I determine if I am leptin resistant? Remember, you can be LR (leptin resistant) if youâ€™re fat or skinny. If youâ€™re overweight by more than 30lbs, it is a lock you have some degree of LR. If youâ€™re underweight by 20 lbs, you are likely LR, too. If you had an eating disorder, youâ€™re likely suffering from a serious leptin issue.
>
> The easiest test is to look in the mirror. The mirror does not lie and it is really cheap. For those people who still canâ€™t be sure after peeking in the mirror, you can order some blood tests. My favorite is the HS CRP (highly sensitive C-Reactive protein) and the reverse T3 tests (but there are others). They are accurate in over 90% of cases.
>
> My next favorite test is the salivary cortisol test, along with at least two of the following: HDL level, serum ferritin, homocysteine, fasting triglycerides, fasting insulin, or post-prandial glucose readings done every 15 minutes for one hour after eating. (Read my Hormone 101 blog for details.)

He doesn't really give details on what to look for when ordering tests.

I'm not overweight - Male, 190 around 15% bf

I've had some of those tests and they come back normal. My HDL is 87, my fasting triglycerides are 75 and fasting insulin is 87.

I'm a firefighter so this may account for some of my issues (mainly sleep).

For the last 9 months my sleep has been terrible with waking multiple times per night, sometimes hard to go back to sleep but usually not.

I do crossfit about 5 times per week along with a paleo diet.

In addition to poor sleep, it seems like I'm always hungry (sometimes I give in sometimes I don't but I pretty don't eat processed sugar) and I have been having odd muscle pains (but I think that might be because of the poor sleep).

My energy has been terrible since the poor sleep but it was never great even before my sleep tanked even with eating strict paleo.

So what else can I do to figure out if I'm leptin resistant or should I just go for the reset considering the info I have provided.

If I do go for the reset there is one main problem that I may have a hard time following: No exercise.

At the fire station we sometimes go about and play football for roughly 3 hrs/week total. Not tackling but quite a bit of sprinting.

I would really lose face if I sat out.

**Thanks for any input you have.**

John

# Thread: Not quite sure if I'm Leptin Resistant - A few questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-quite-sure-if-im-leptin-resistant-a-few-questions.7173/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-05-28T06:09:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-quite-sure-if-im-leptin-resistant-a-few-questions.7173/post-87000>

Was just reading Jack's epic blog post on leptin resistance:
http://www.jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/

Just reading the section on determining if you're leptin resistant where he says:

> How do I determine if I am leptin resistant? Remember, you can be LR (leptin resistant) if youâ€™re fat or skinny. If youâ€™re overweight by more than 30lbs, it is a lock you have some degree of LR. If youâ€™re underweight by 20 lbs, you are likely LR, too. If you had an eating disorder, youâ€™re likely suffering from a serious leptin issue.
>
> The easiest test is to look in the mirror. The mirror does not lie and it is really cheap. For those people who still canâ€™t be sure after peeking in the mirror, you can order some blood tests. My favorite is the HS CRP (highly sensitive C-Reactive protein) and the reverse T3 tests (but there are others). They are accurate in over 90% of cases.
>
> My next favorite test is the salivary cortisol test, along with at least two of the following: HDL level, serum ferritin, homocysteine, fasting triglycerides, fasting insulin, or post-prandial glucose readings done every 15 minutes for one hour after eating. (Read my Hormone 101 blog for details.)

He doesn't really give details on what to look for when ordering tests.

I'm not overweight - Male, 190 around 15% bf

I've had some of those tests and they come back normal. My HDL is 87, my fasting triglycerides are 75 and fasting insulin is 87.

I'm a firefighter so this may account for some of my issues (mainly sleep).

For the last 9 months my sleep has been terrible with waking multiple times per night, sometimes hard to go back to sleep but usually not.

I do crossfit about 5 times per week along with a paleo diet.

In addition to poor sleep, it seems like I'm always hungry (sometimes I give in sometimes I don't but I pretty don't eat processed sugar) and I have been having odd muscle pains (but I think that might be because of the poor sleep).

My energy has been terrible since the poor sleep but it was never great even before my sleep tanked even with eating strict paleo.

So what else can I do to figure out if I'm leptin resistant or should I just go for the reset considering the info I have provided.

If I do go for the reset there is one main problem that I may have a hard time following: No exercise.

At the fire station we sometimes go about and play football for roughly 3 hrs/week total. Not tackling but quite a bit of sprinting.

I would really lose face if I sat out.

**Thanks for any input you have.**

John

# Thread: A few questions before I (re)Start
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-questions-before-i-re-start.7175/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-05-28T17:18:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-questions-before-i-re-start.7175/post-87014>

I guess I should have mentioned this in my first thread but totally forgot.

I mentioned in my other thread that my sleep really went to crap about 9 months ago.

What I forgot to mention is that I tried the Leptin Rx 9 months ago and did it for roughly 3 weeks.

That's when my sleep tanked and the only thing I could figure was that it had something to do with the low carb and or high fat of the Rx.

I finally gave up and noticed some relief but it has never been the same since.

Basically when I gave up because I would wake up in the middle of the night and have a really hard time going back to sleep, sometimes I wouldn't be able to go back to sleep at at all after 3 or 4 hours of sleep.

Since I quit the Reset I now wake up several times during the night but usually don't have much trouble going back to sleep.

I'm concerned that if I try this again that the sleep will get worse again.

I should also mention that I've tried nearly everything for sleep - melatonin more than anything with no real improvements.

I just ordered 2 supplements from Amazon that Greg Everett (buddy of Robb Wolf) said has helped him:
Holy Basil and Gaba but maybe I should send those back.

One more question:

If I give this another go is regular cured bacon okay or does it need to be uncured?

I ask because sugar is in the ingredients list of the cured but it's not enough be even a gram per serving.

At this time I just can't afford the price of uncured bacon.

Thank you so much for your input.

John
