> Steam distilled water is not wonderful. Steam vapor is high in protons so the condensate is acidic. Read Gerald Pollack's new book regarding the nature of evaporation. Maybe putting the water in a grounded vessel might give it some electrons.
>
> I would hope that everyone knows that using bottled water on a regular basis is insane for a number of reasons.

If I recall some of Pollack's work, one MIGHT be able to make NZ water by adding a collagen or a liquid protein to water and then shining light on it. Did not Pollack's work show that protein membranes appear to generate a structured water with a strong negative charge under infrared light.

### Jonathin — 2013-06-11T19:55:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/affordable-options-for-flouride-free-water-ideas.7306/post-87970>

"Please post few"

consumer expense - I would rather spend my money on more or better food
container pollution - recycling is great but does not happen everywhere
transportation / energy expenditure - water weighs alot
truth in labeling / quality - do you trust a label? Do you trust that there won't be bottling errors?
security - how many days of water are you going to have on hand before going to the store again

### Jonathin — 2013-06-11T20:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/affordable-options-for-flouride-free-water-ideas.7306/post-87971>

> [GlenPDQ said:](/goto/post?id=87956)
>
> If I recall some of Pollack's work, one MIGHT be able to make NZ water by adding a collagen or a liquid protein to water and then shining light on it. Did not Pollack's work show that protein membranes appear to generate a structured water with a strong negative charge under infrared light.

Hydrophillic surfaces (usually with a negative charge) are a seed of charge that allows structured water to build layers like layers of hexagonal chicken wire. Energy in the form of infrared light allows the expansion of the structured layer (exclusion zone or EZ layer). Any number of interfacial situations can create a structured water to various degrees, i.e. the surface of a lake to the inside of a nano-tube. There seem to be a number of water structures that can be artificially generated with magnets, vortexes, ultrasound. I think it is still be determined the value or biological effects of these types of water.

A little off topic:
For me as a farmer I am fascinated by the structuring of water in a soil that has a high level of microbiological activity and clay-humus complexes. This in coordination with photosynthetic plants that pump carbon nutrients through their roots into the soil creates an expanding soil zone of high energy and ehanced nutrient availability. A healthy soil is a work in progress like our own personal health.

### Grizz — 2013-10-02T14:53:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/affordable-options-for-flouride-free-water-ideas.7306/post-96693>

> [Ltwo said:](/goto/post?id=96667)
>
> Has anyone seen any water test results for Primo water?

This is a GREAT topic. Here is the water analysis:
[http://www.primowater.com/PrimoNew/files/92/92ac9e80-5937-4ce2-8a8f-666816d8aed3.pdf](http://www.primowater.com/PrimoNew/files/92/92ac9e80-5937-4ce2-8a8f-666816d8aed3.pdf)

According to EPA estimates, 40 million Americans are exposed to levels of lead in water well above the EPA’s proposed maximum contaminant allowances.
(U.S. Tap Water Report released by the Environmental Working Group)
Primo is lead free, chlorine free, fluoride free and pretty much everything else free!
[http://www.primowaterdirect.com/Our-Water/Water-Facts](http://www.primowaterdirect.com/Our-Water/Water-Facts)

Notice: NO fluoride and that is where most of the water filters fail. The only 2 methods that are effective is reverse osmosis & distilled water. $1.37 for 3 gallons is about the cost of electricity to distill 3 gallons at 10 cents per Kwh. In most areas of the USA, Primo is less expensive than the electricity to distill water !

Primowater is made via reverse osmosis filters.
[http://www.primowater.com/Great-Value-FILL-IT-YOURSELF.aspx](http://www.primowater.com/Great-Value-FILL-IT-YOURSELF.aspx)

Grizz

# Thread: How to recover from anorexia?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 53
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hemming — 2013-06-07T20:38:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-87745>

I'm a 26y old male, 5'9''. I've been having anorexia for around one year and lost weight from 143lbs to 109lbs at my lowest, I'm now at 116lbs. I ran >70k/week, biked 40k/week and did some weight exercises in a large part of that period and then burned out. At the moment I bike 45min each day to and from work and do some body weight exercises and free weight exercises 3-5 times/week.

I just had some labs done and almost all of it is in the normal range. My zinc and red blood platelets are too low and my amylase is too high, my TSH is 1.34 (I know that doesn't say much alone) it has fallen from above 2 since my last test. In December I had the following tests (SI units)
LDL 3.4
HDL 1.62
Tri 0.82
CRP <0.2 (it doesn't say HS)

I'm experiencing all the symptoms of low cortisol. Generally I'm very fatigued, my legs feel like I've ran a marathon, I'm unable to concentrate, indecisive and very confused about almost everything.

I've been eating paleo for eight months but didn't learn about this site until a couple of weeks ago. I eat a ketogenic diet but have a fear of protein (besides food in general) as I'm scared of the side effects of eating too much protein (even though none of blood samples show any indications of this). Recently I've also become afraid of fat and try to restrict that too - I'm contemplating that that might have something to do with low energy level too as I'm simply not eating enough. But when I try to eat more fat I get scared of my lipids and when I try to eat more carbs I get scared of all the adverse effects from that.

Basically my question is how would it be best to recover and get my former energy, body and strength back? Any concrete advice on macro ratios would be greatly appreciated. The food is self explanatory but how should I balance carbs, protein and fat? Should I just go all out on eating as much as possible for the three big meals, should I follow the BAB protocol? How many calories should i eat?

The food is self explanatory but how should I balance carbs, protein and fat? Should I just go all out on eating as much as possible for the three big meals, should I follow the BAB protocol? How many calories should i eat?

I remember a few anorexic and bulimic people were successful with BAB. You don't count calories on BAB. You eat low carb, high protein and high fat until you have no food cravings.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-09T15:37:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-87834>

with anorexia you must go high fat moderate protein and moderate carb slowly and steadily........and things will usually come back in your hypothalamus. You must use the diet and sun light to re signal the correct signal to the brain for it to rewire. Anorexia is a forced change to the neural wiring of the hypothalamus that must be re done using neuroplasticity. The best way to monitor recovery is your hormone panel........and monitoring your cravings......we want you to re gain them and this is a function of NPY and dopamine.........it just takes time and you "want to"

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-09T15:37:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-87835>

How big is your "WANT TO" ? Do you have a passionate desire to accomplish something? There are many ways to get to your goal but you will never get there without the "will". "Will" directs your passion and is the faculty by which you decides upon and initiates action. A person with a big "WANT TO" don't read history to get encouraged, there are so driven that they make history. A person with a big "WANT TO" will
use failures as stepping stones and rejections fall off like water on a duck's back. People with a big "WANT TO" have seen the end result already and will never be satisfied unless the find a way to reach their vision. These men and women are relentless and unstoppable because their vision consumes them. People with a big "WANT TO" are not waiting to be validated , pampered, patted on the back. They are self motivated and fearless to stand alone . They will take life by its horn and aren't afraid to walk the road alone because that's the right thing to do. Today consider the power of your "will" to succeed in any endeavor, because you can climb any mountain and overcome any ceiling if you have a "Big Want to".

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-09T17:42:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-87842>

All this………can be explained from understand how dopamine fits into epigentics……..some say its complicated…………Well, I value my life so I will love chaos and complexity. Optimal dopamine balace allows for the proper choices and ultimate survival for humans. This truth is hard wired into our central nervous system. Bad choices that limit our survival are also born of lowering our brain dopamine levels. Never forget……….when we know better we do better period end of report. Dopamine is how we chose and make choice correctly for wellness.

In QB 9 I showed you precisely what CT is at its core for survival........cold increases Oxygen tension in tissues of all land based and marine based mammals.......the more o2 in your tissues the more efficient your mitochondria work. This is precisely what HBO does artificially. Cold does it naturally. People with anorexia have lived their life prior to getting the disease doing a ton of things wrong........all of which act to lower dopamine and this caused them to re wire their hypothalamus ot perceive the environment in an altered reality fashion.........the goal now is not to worry about how they see the world.......it is to force their brains to see the world as it really appears with respect to sun water and food and re train their inner mitochondrial membrane to signal correctly the epigenetic environment they really live in.......not the one they perceived as the disease gained control of their neuroplastic abilities.......

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-09T17:45:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-87843>

"want to" is a function of dopamine levels and not someone will power; and dopamine levels are proxy for how well you use the correct signals of the photoelectric effect........anorexia and obesity at their core are the same disease, but with different environmental signals being sent to the hypothalamus leading to two sets of reality............what we "think" is two separate conditions........they are not. They are one and the same. If you listen to this months webinar.........I discuss how this effect happens in vision too.........and I use an example of a GSW to prove it.

The same is true for a normal person and a diabetic. A normal person can eat a snickers bar without too much risk.......and diabetic can not. Why? ### Dextery — 2013-06-15T21:41:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-88125>

> I'm seeing my doctor at a specialist clinic Monday, any tests I should ask for that relates specifically to anorexia in addition to the ones you generally recommend and cortisol? I'll be asking for HS-CRP, lipid panel and thyroid panel.

If you do not ask for a reverse T3 test along with the normal thyroid panel, most likely you will not get it. Go to [www.stopthethroidmadness.com](http://www.stopthethroidmadness.com) in advance of your visit to your doctor to learn about T3/RT3 ratios. If they will do it, also get a NMR lipid profile done. Most importantly, it will tell you your total LDL particle number and your small dense LDL number. You want that number to be around 10% or less. Report back.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-30T21:17:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-89248>

Eat the protein and fat........and no protein wont kill you.....plus it helps increase carnosine which is big for those with Eating disorders like Anorexia, bulemia, and T2D. the issue is you need a lot of carnosine to offset the glycation in your inner mitochondrial membrane that is slowing down your current. If your hunger is returning that is an excellent prognostic sign that your hypothalamus can rewire and you did not fry your hypocretin neurons.......

### Hemming — 2013-06-30T21:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-89250>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=89248)
>
> Eat the protein and fat........and no protein wont kill you.....plus it helps increase carnosine which is big for those with Eating disorders like Anorexia, bulemia, and T2D. the issue is you need a lot of carnosine to offset the glycation in your inner mitochondrial membrane that is slowing down your current. If your hunger is returning that is an excellent prognostic sign that your hypothalamus can rewire and you did not fry your hypocretin neurons.......

Thanks a lot for the concise answer Jack! I'm happy to hear that protein is actually more beneficial for me as it's something I've been fearing for a while.

I'm also happy that my increased hunger is a sign of recovery and that I should just respond to it by eating more protein and fat. My biggest problem now will be actually responding to that hunger without feeling guilty - which is why is so good to get that information from you to get some comfort in what I'm doing is 'the right thing'. As a follow-up question - could my continued chronic fatigue be because I'm still not eating enough in general?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-01T02:43:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-89264>

could be do to a lot......but recoveries are based on stochastic math........and therefore nothing in recovery is linear, except your belief that recoveries are in fact linear.

### Hemming — 2013-07-01T11:31:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-recover-from-anorexia.7308/post-89287>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=89264)
>
> could be do to a lot......but recoveries are based on stochastic math........and therefore nothing in recovery is linear, except your belief that recoveries are in fact linear.

Thanks Jack. Ironically, I've actually studied stochastic math so I should be able to understand it except that this mental state makes you confused about even the most simple things.

Reading more and more of your blog I see that almost all the answers are there - it's just about getting to them.

# Thread: High Carbs In The Summertime, What?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carbs-in-the-summertime-what.7310/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-06-07T22:04:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carbs-in-the-summertime-what.7310/post-87750>

Can someone explain or point me to where JK explains why he eats high carb in the summertime?

I was listening to his recent podcast with Ben and after saying that carbs are not needed for performance he went on to say that in parts of June, July and August he eats over 250 grams of carbs per day.

I'm a bit confused.

If a performance athlete doesn't need them why would anyone else?

### Hemming — 2013-06-07T22:28:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carbs-in-the-summertime-what.7310/post-87756>

He eats them because they're available in abundance during the summer time. He eats seasonally.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-07T23:53:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carbs-in-the-summertime-what.7310/post-87764>

carbs are only designed to grow in long light cycles when the photoelectric effect is massive. Read EMF 7 and the QB 7-9 to get this point......it is massively important.

### Danco3636 — 2013-06-08T00:14:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carbs-in-the-summertime-what.7310/post-87768>

I've also heard Jack mention that to express the PPP for performance and speed up the adaptions to remain keto or VLC throught the summer or long light cycles. This is what I am doing.

### sjoshua — 2013-06-08T01:36:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carbs-in-the-summertime-what.7310/post-87770>

I believe your compliance depends on what you are hoping to take away. If I'm correct, Jack's ultimate goal is optimized health, which would dictate following the seasonal food availability trends.

But, like Danco said, if your goal is achieving performance and improving your body's fat adaptation then consuming ample carbs following seasonal trends would be working against you, hence a performance-minded individual would be more likely to remain in/around ketosis.

### Dali Dula — 2013-06-08T02:06:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carbs-in-the-summertime-what.7310/post-87772>

> [sjoshua said:](/goto/post?id=87770)
>
> I believe your compliance depends on what you are hoping to take away. If I'm correct, Jack's ultimate goal is optimized health, which would dictate following the seasonal food availability trends.
>
> But, like Danco said, if your goal is achieving performance and improving your body's fat adaptation then consuming ample carbs following seasonal trends would be working against you, hence a performance-minded individual would be more likely to remain in/around ketosis.

This would also apply to anyone with broken glucose metabolism-diabetics. I manage tight control with T2D and will not change my carb consumption much in high light cycles. I plan on picking and eating wild berries and possibly some pears and peaches ripe off the tree. That's it.

### fireforlunch — 2013-06-08T02:30:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carbs-in-the-summertime-what.7310/post-87775>

Thank you Jack

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=87764)

# Thread: BAB, leptin reset, and chronic hunger...please chime in
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-leptin-reset-and-chronic-hunger-please-chime-in.7317/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bennyha — 2013-06-09T02:05:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-leptin-reset-and-chronic-hunger-please-chime-in.7317/post-87816>

Hello all. I have a question regarding the Leptin Reset, the BAB, and hunger. I have been doing the Leptin Reset for the past year or so. I am 6'0 and 155 lbs and probably 12% body fat or so. My hormones are out of whack, and my Reverse T3 is elevated. I also used to be a binge eater, and I think Jack said those with eating disorders in the past take longer to become LS. I'm thinking maybe I haven't become LS yet because I'm not doing it entirely correctly. The problem is, I NEVER feel full. So I will stop my BAB at 3,000 calories because it just seems like I will do more harm than good (digestion and gut wise) if I eat more than that. During my binge days, I could eat up to 10,000 calories in a single day-no joke! Those days are behind me, but I still feel hungry in the morning despite eating a huge breakfast.

My typical breakfast is 6 large eggs, 8 oz grass fed ground beef, 4 TBS coconut oil, 4 TBS lard, 1 TBS olive oil, and some veggies. I don't tolerate butter, as I break out after I eat it, just I now just avoid it.

My questions are:

1) Should I just keep eating until I somehow feel full, even if that means eating an entire jar of coconut oil in one setting? I have gained 7 lbs in the past 2 weeks eating large amounts trying to tame the hunger, but it just won't subside no matter how much I eat. Fat seems to not fill me up one bit. I could eat a huge tub of lard and not feel full.

2) Is it possible to be leptin sensitive if you feel constantly hungry? I have chronic fatigue for the most part, so it's almost as if the food I eat isn't fueling me.

3) If yes to eating to satiety, how high should I go? I have an enormous appetite for my size, and could maybe go up to 6,000 calories or so. How should I add my calories? I'm assuming fat.

Thank you for any help you can give me

### Dextery — 2013-06-09T02:39:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-leptin-reset-and-chronic-hunger-please-chime-in.7317/post-87817>

It sounds like your ghrelin hormone that tells you that you are hungry is in runaway mode. The Leptin hormone plays the role to tell your brain hypothalamus that you are no longer hungry or satiated. Leptin resistance can account for this. Ghrelin is overwhelming Leptin. Become leptin sensitive and your cravings probably will abate.

[http://www.obesityaction.org/educat...les-2/general-articles/ghrelin-the-go-hormone](http://www.obesityaction.org/educational-resources/resource-articles-2/general-articles/ghrelin-the-go-hormone)

Have you ever done a 24 hour fast? You know you will not die by not eating for 24 hours. Try it and see how you do. Remember, you are totally responsible for what you eat regardless of what the gremlin hormone GHRELIN says to you. Train yourself not to listen to GHRELIN.

### Inger — 2013-06-09T06:30:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-leptin-reset-and-chronic-hunger-please-chime-in.7317/post-87824>

I see you guys as a former bulemic and binge eater... I also need quite big amounts of food and fat alone does not really fill me up either. I need lots of protein for breakfast. I can eat one pound of raw elk meat easily and it has so much protein because it has so little fat in it.
The only thing I can suggest is.. what if you try to eat the amount of food in, say, mussels? I am curious to see what would happen. Or what if you try to eat the ground beef raw.. and the eggs too, or ditch the eggs all together? Hubs like to eat beef tartar once in a while, but he can eat quite a small amount only and he is satiated, way sooner than with cooked meat. IDK why. But you could try. Try to eat the salmon raw too. 1 pound of raw salmon is very satiating to me. You could benefit from eating the food raw. At least I think it would be worth a try...

I would say Dextery could be right, in such extreme situations it could be good to use your brain to limit the food if the raw thing does not help. I cannot eat as much as I want because I have not money for it. But I am not going hungry either. I am also only a small woman.. so you guys should eat the double amount of what I eat = 2 pounds of raw ground elk. With lots of coconut oil on the top. I really wonder if you would still be hungry after...

Another thing, maybe your body craves micronutrients? That is why fat does not cut it either, because it has no selenium, zink, Iodine.. etc. That is what I tell myself when I am kind of shocked how much meat I can eat. It might take a long time to fill up the bodys reserves. Supplements might not cut it at all as they not always get assimilated.

### HoneyChild — 2013-06-09T12:26:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-leptin-reset-and-chronic-hunger-please-chime-in.7317/post-87829>

Are you doing CT? Eventually CT should help reduce your hunger.

Hopefully somehow, someway things will start to change. The "want to" for me is there, perhaps too much. I've been dealing with this for over 2 years now. Also, should someone in my position up the carbs some like he told her to do? I am unsure of this. I have been eating less than 20 grams of carbs for months, feel lousy all the time

Bennyha,

I havent been suffering with the extreme hunger as long as you have, but have come to the point where I can literally eat a whole jar of Coconut oil with 70 grams of protein and still be hungry, I've even resorted to frinking olive oil from the bottle while eating macadamia nuts. ### bennyha — 2013-06-11T18:56:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-leptin-reset-and-chronic-hunger-please-chime-in.7317/post-87967>

Hey LRichard. I posted this just a few days ago, but I have been dealing with these problems for almost 3 years now. Seemingly out of nowhere, I completely lost my hunger signal, started binge eating/fasting fiasco, and all my health problems started to domino shortly after. It's been a long adventure and one that I don't wish upon my worst enemy. I continue to put on weight, but eating 5,000 calories a day will do that. I am just hoping that I can somehow become LS. The way I understand it, as long as one is LR, then it is impossible to have good health and heal. So I am pushing the calories and fat to see if that helps. I have been doing the Leptin Reset for nearly two years now, and still haven't had success killing my hunger.

I ate 3,500+ calories for breakfast this morning alone-1 lbs ground beef, 4 eggs, 4 pieces bacon, 5 TBS lard, 14 TBS! CO. For whatever reason, I just don't get full on fat and protein. Haven't for the past few years. I have mainly been very low carb for the most part the past couple years as well, and that is when all my problems began as well. I know carbs are evil, but I do feel better physically and mentally as well as from a satiety perspective as well when I eat between 50-100 grams of carbs a day. I know they are bad in long run, so I'm trying to just keep on keeping on eating very low carb.

Have you had any hormone testing? Or even simply measured your temperature? I believe that elevated rT3 can cause problems with overeating, fatigue etc. Seemingly a lot of people have "type 2 hypothyroidism" where TSH and T4 are normal but their T3 is low and rT3 is high. Grizz posted a video about this and how you need various things such as selenium, iodine and progesterone to make the conversion from T4 to T3

\[video=youtube;SzCryUF050U\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player\_embedded&v=SzCryUF050U#\[/video\]

It's not mentioned in the video but a B12 deficiency can also cause elevated rT3.

> [HoneyChild said:](/goto/post?id=87994)
>
> Have you had any hormone testing? Or even simply measured your temperature? I believe that elevated rT3 can cause problems with overeating, fatigue etc. Seemingly a lot of people have "type 2 hypothyroidism" where TSH and T4 are normal but their T3 is low and rT3 is high. Grizz posted a video about this and how you need various things such as selenium, iodine and progesterone to make the conversion from T4 to T3
>
> \[video=youtube;SzCryUF050U\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player\_embedded&v=SzCryUF050U#\[/video\]
>
> It's not mentioned in the video but a B12 deficiency can also cause elevated rT3.

I have had some hormone testing over the past year or two. I have low Free T3, a really high TSH, low Free T4 (but barely in range). My RT3 was high but in range, but my FT3:RT3 ratio was well under 20. My temps are all over the place, and vary based on my settings, but run about 1 degree low. Also, my heart rate is always between 50-60. The fact I can't sweat for 2 years now and have an extreme sensitivity to cold tells me things are wrong. I have more adrenal fatigue symptoms though. I believe my thyroid is dysfunctional because my wiring is way off. I used to binge eat and fast for days at a time. I went from high to low cortisol. I have been trying all this stuff for 2 years and have found no relief. I am at odds about what to do next...nothing seems to work.

But aren't low thyroid labs just a leptin problem?

Can one be leptin sensitive when having low thyroid lab numbers? That's my real question.

Could some of my perceived leptin problems and hunger problems be solved if I went on thyroid medicine to get my thyroid optimized? So confused on this...

### HoneyChild — 2013-06-14T14:37:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-leptin-reset-and-chronic-hunger-please-chime-in.7317/post-88083>

>
> Can one be leptin sensitive when having low thyroid lab numbers? That's my real question.
>

Leptin problems can certainly result in hypothyroidism but I still think that nutritional deficiencies such as iodine, selenium and B12 can cause problems too and need to be resolved. Jack has said a number of times that hormone replacement might be needed for a lot of people because our environment has altered so much we need need to do everything we can to fight back.

For me personally, the only health problem I really have is zero libido and when I got my sex hormones tested they came back very low. I've been trying my best with diet, cold baths and nutritional supplements but I have a sinking feeling that I'll need to start buying what I can over the counter - estrogen, progesterone and dhea. I haven't had my thyroid tested but I'm on the iodine protocol just now. Will hopefully get my thyroid tested soon.

Lack of sweating can be leptin resistance but I watched a Youtube video of Dr Jorge Flechas talking about how iodine deficiency can do this too.

Destiny posted this on the thyroid thread:-

> [Destiny said:](/goto/post?id=87724)
>
> I had to drop my T3 medication even more since I started taking seven 12.5 mg Iodoral every day. I am at a 1/3 of the previous dosage. I guess my thyroid is kicking in?

A lot of people here are on thyroid/sex hormones in addition to all the nutritional/lifestyle protocols Jack has laid out. I set out to heal myself through diet/lifestyle but I'm just not sure if this is possible considering what we've all been doing to ourselves over the years. For me personally I was vegetarian most of my life and was brought up thinking that fat, salt and sun are all bad for you. Then combine this with artificial light and EMFs... things aren't going to fix themselves quickly.

I think you should seriously consider going to a doctor in order to get thyroid meds. Perhaps over time your body will heal itself and you'll not need to keep taking the meds forever.

I have been feeling awful for 3 years now, and at 31 with no libido at all, I'm "wasting" what are suppose to be the best years of my life. I, like you, have tried all these protocols, but they have just not been enough. # Thread: Grandma's Experiences Leave a Mark on Your Genes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grandmas-experiences-leave-a-mark-on-your-genes.7318/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dextery — 2013-06-09T22:50:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grandmas-experiences-leave-a-mark-on-your-genes.7318/post-87861>

[http://discovermagazine.com/2013/ma...ve-epigenetic-mark-on-your-genes#.UbSjy5xkkSn](http://discovermagazine.com/2013/may/13-grandmas-experiences-leave-epigenetic-mark-on-your-genes#.UbSjy5xkkSn)

**Your ancestors' lousy childhoods or excellent adventures might change your personality, bequeathing anxiety or resilience by altering the epigenetic expressions of genes in the brain.**

"Szyf and Meaney considered a hypothesis as improbable as it was profound: If diet and chemicals can cause epigenetic changes, could certain experiences â€” child neglect, drug abuse or other severe stresses â€” also set off epigenetic changes to the DNA inside the neurons of a personâ€™s brain? That question turned out to be the basis of a new field, behavioral epigenetics, now so vibrant it has spawned dozens of studies and suggested profound new treatments to heal the brain."

Dr Kruse has talked about the genes of our grandmothers that are passed on influence the being of their children and grandchildren. But I did not understand that how the grandmother experienced life... say whether a hard cruel life or a life of affluence...affects how the children and grandchildren express their genes. Epigenetics of behavior...a loving, touching, caring mother passes along those epigenetic genes to the offspring...& to the grandchild. The offspring will indeed receive these traits. Likewise the rock hardened life of a cruel mother with little or no loving or caring will pass along those epigenetic genes to the child and grandchildren.

The offspring of rats that were shy and reticent were switched to a mother rat that exhibited outgoing social behavior did not change their epigenetic expression of reticence...nor did their offspring.

Very interesting read. You never knew how important your grandmother was/is to who you are.

There are some on this forum that say they have never met a challenge they did not like and always are upbeat and exuberant - and there are others that say they are somewhat a loner and not outgoing but are doing quite well. Some of our greatest thinkers have been shy and retiring.

Can anyone identify how their grandmother affected their state of being today?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-09T23:03:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grandmas-experiences-leave-a-mark-on-your-genes.7318/post-87862>

Read about the Dutch winter experiment from WW2.......it details this perfectly.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-09T23:03:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grandmas-experiences-leave-a-mark-on-your-genes.7318/post-87863>

I wrote about it in the Evolutionary Friend or Foe blog.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-10T00:09:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grandmas-experiences-leave-a-mark-on-your-genes.7318/post-87868>

Epigenetics blog links:
BRAIN GUT 3: LOOK IN THE PAST TO SEE YOUR PROLOGUE
BRAIN GUT 2: VIRAL MARKETING
CPC #5 THE LEAKY GUT/ADRENAL FATIGUE CASE
E=MC2: MAKING FACTOR X ACTIONABLE
CPC#4 EVOLUTIONARY FRIEND OR FOE?
WHY PERSPECTIVE MATTERS?
THE "TEETH" IN DISEASE?
AVOIDING FATES OF RONALD REAGAN OR MICHAEL J FOX.
DEVELOPMENTAL ORIGINS OF DISEASE

### caroline — 2013-06-10T05:33:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grandmas-experiences-leave-a-mark-on-your-genes.7318/post-87895>

My maternal grandmother was a very cold, non demonstrative woman ..... my mother was the same - I only remember being hugged once in all my growing up years .... consequently I craved love and affection above all else ... but had a hard time accepting it.
Thank God for the love of a very loving, kind, generous and extra ordinarily patient man..... who loved me more than anything or anyone .... and I have never understood why....

# Thread: An interesting read...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-interesting-read.7320/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-10T02:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-interesting-read.7320/post-87873>

[http://archaeonova.blogspot.com/2013/04/myths-of-paleo-part-one.html](http://archaeonova.blogspot.com/2013/04/myths-of-paleo-part-one.html)

Just recently read this, in fact, I'm about to go and re-read it. For those of you who are familiar with most/a lot of Dr. Kruse' blogs, this may not be entirely new to you. I was using it as a reference in my opinion about vegetables and carbs over at the Bulletproof Exec Forum. It delves into our evolution from what Dr. Kruse would call our "aquatic ape ancestors," and why starches make absolutely no sense in the human diet. The author goes on to cite how many times starches and legumes were actually applied in various ways other than a nutritional staple (using them for shelter and such). She is obviously an advocate of an ultra-high fat, very low carb, moderate-moderately low protein diet.

This actually inspired me even more now to tap into the PPP. I have been also following a keto-oriented thread over at MDA and saw how quickly some people could bounce back into ketosis after a "binge" or they maintained ketosis even after increasing protein. Kind of gives merit to the whole "protein loading" idea, especially when used in combination with MCTs.

# Thread: Should I get this CardioScan
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-get-this-cardioscan.7323/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-06-10T16:47:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-get-this-cardioscan.7323/post-87915>

Tomorrow I have bloodwork scheduled at the prompting of my cardiologist that will look at the large particle vs small particle of my LDL because.

She wants to do it because my LDL is in the 280's.

But my HDL is in the 80's and my triglycerides are 94.

In two weeks when I go to get the results of the LDL bloodwork she wants me to do a CaredioScan that will show a picture of any plaque in my arteries.

It's not covered by insurance so it's going to cost me $50 out of pocket.

Is this necessary?

I'd rather put the money towards something like a cortisol test or a test that JK recommends.

Thanks for your input.

### nonchalant — 2013-06-10T16:55:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-get-this-cardioscan.7323/post-87917>

I think the particle size test is not necessary, but it is good because it will make your doctor feel better about your not taking statins.
If the particles are nice and fluffy, as they will be because of what your HDL & trig levels indicate, I'm not sure that a scan would be necessary.
Personally I'd like to have a scan, to reassure my relatives that are shocked at the amount of animal fat I consume. But I also would rather use the funds on a more useful test.

### nonchalant — 2013-06-10T16:59:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-get-this-cardioscan.7323/post-87918>

Wait, a CardioScan is a type of CT scan? Sounds like a lot of EMF...
Perhaps the cardiologist is being pressured to make more use out of the CT machine.

### Dextery — 2013-06-10T23:06:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-get-this-cardioscan.7323/post-87939>

I'm with nonchalant on the emfs from the ct scan. You are 35 and in good shape as a firefighter should be. Heart calcification should not be a problem at your age. Is it just your LDL number? But that said a heart CT scan for $50 is very cheap.

The blood work will let you know where you are at. The NMR test will tell you your total LDL particle number(LDL-P)and your sm dense LDL particle number and then you will know that...hopefully the sm dense are 10% or less of the total LDL particle number.
And the LDL-C of 280s doesn't matter if they are all large bouyant.
The number that jumps out at me is your triglycerides. That number needs to be down in the 40s. With the NMR you will also find out your HDL-C number and that should be around 80 or above....the higher the better.

Be prepared for a particle number to be much bigger than the less than 1000 nmol/L number reference range indicated. And be prepared for a total cholesterol to be much higher than the 200mg/dl reference range.

Our paleo/epi-paleo diet does raise total cholesterol and HDL and LDL but the fat we eat...especially the saturated fat coconut oil & MCTs are good for the body.

I would bet she is going to freak out and tell you she is very concerned with your blood work. LOL.

Post your results.

### fireforlunch — 2013-06-11T03:09:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-get-this-cardioscan.7323/post-87953>

Thank you for posting, I feel good about holding off.

I will get the cardio scan yearly at work when I turn 40.

What is the difference between HDL and HDL-C?

My HDL is 87

>

> And the LDL-C of 280s doesn't matter if they are all large bouyant.
> The number that jumps out at me is your triglycerides. That number needs to be down in the 40s. With the NMR you will also find out your HDL-C number and that should be around 80 or above....the higher the better.

>

>
> I would bet she is going to freak out and tell you she is very concerned with your blood work. LOL.

### Dextery — 2013-06-11T03:39:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-get-this-cardioscan.7323/post-87955>

# Thread: Leaky Gut/AI - Chicken and Pork?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-ai-chicken-and-pork.7326/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kposs — 2013-06-10T18:24:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-ai-chicken-and-pork.7326/post-87924>

Hi,

I don't actually have a diagnosed AI, but I definitely have leaky gut and systemic inflammation from chronic NSAID use. I did so well not eating eggs (almost 40 point drop in HSCRP - yes, it was THAT high) that I'm currently doing a super strict Epi-Paleo Leaky Gut/AI protocol (no eggs, soy, dairy, grains, nuts, legumes, nightshades, poultry, pork, chocolate, processed meats, seeds, etc.) for 6 weeks (to start) until my next blood tests.

I've searched, but I cannot find a definitive answer about chicken and pork and any reasoning as to why they might be good or bad for leaky gut/AI. Obviously not epi-paleo to start, but just wondering for sake of variety down the road.

thanks!

### Dextery — 2013-06-10T18:51:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-ai-chicken-and-pork.7326/post-87929>

Dr K has said fowl is fowl and he does not eat fowl. Most of us eat bacon, ham, pork chops etc. But if you had a hsCRP that is supposed to be under .5 mg/L stay with the epi-paleo nutrient dense seafoods in the order Dr. K outlined.

You can use copious amounts of turmeric as a gut healing agent. Buy it in bulk for about $8 a pound rather than buying capsules.

Make bone broth to also heal the gut.

Looking forward to your next report on hsCRP and blood tests.

### mh586 — 2013-06-12T06:34:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-ai-chicken-and-pork.7326/post-87988>

The argument against pork and poultry it the high omega 6 fat content, specifically when grain fed. PUFAs increase intestinal permeability (i.e. cause leaky gut) while saturated fat improves it.

# Thread: Cycling
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycling.7334/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### WuLingZhi — 2013-06-12T11:38:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycling.7334/post-87989>

Aloha all, first post. I'm a recreational cyclist, and I've been riding for about a year and a half, with sometime off during a collarbone fracture. I live in hawaii, so I also surf a good bit too. I've been eating a mixture of epi, and bulletproof the past two to three months, and feel great. My weekly average is around 150-200 miles with some heavy climbing. My questions are concerned with what I need to keep my performance up with some increased training. Ie, carb intake, protein, supplements etc. I have been eating under 75gm of carbs, and around 150gm of protein a day. Bulletproof coffee and the mct oil. I take a mixture of supplements, mostly Chinese adaptogens, as I'm a masters degree student in his last year for acupuncture, and integrative medicine. I also take the normal stacks of b vitamins, bcaa, magnesium, iodine, iron, etc. I know my cortisol levels are good, considering I'm a student trying to study for board exams, and doing chronic cardio. Hormone panels are all normal as well. I'm 29, 6'2" and around 200 lbs. Basically I've looked through the threads and found some interesting stuff from Barry, but a lot of it is very anecdotal. Any other cyclists out there? If so what are you guys eating? I'm very interested in the PPP, but either I've missed a detailed regime on how to access it, or it's not available, thanks!

### receo — 2013-10-04T10:17:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycling.7334/post-96859>

Cycling are best to burn your legs fat and strong your lega bone.I also take the normal stacks of b vitamins, bcaa, magnesium, iodine, iron, etc. I know my cortisol levels are good, considering I'm a student trying to study for board exams, and doing chronic cardio.

### MeghanK — 2013-10-04T19:15:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycling.7334/post-96879>

I do a little higher carb closer to 100g a day.... and train 2-3 hours a day mixture of run, cycle, strength, cross-fit etc... but I'm also female and many studies are coming out that females might need to be higher carb.... I would make yourself an N=1.... try higher carb 150-250g for 2-3 weeks keeping your cycling regime the same (do you have a power meter?)....and throw in a "test" course for time... then do 2-3 weeks of low carb same routine... I would do the low carb one first since you have been pretty low carb .... then compare your power/HR/ stress/ HRV/ feeling of fatigue in general etc.....
What do you eat to get your 75g carbs?
Do you take Creatine?

### Renfred — 2013-10-11T07:04:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycling.7334/post-97501>

Cycling is great to lose unwanted weight, boost stamina and increase coordination. Cycling build strength, tone muscles and improves heart health. Cycling prevent from obesity, hypertension, heart disease and certain types of cancer.
[click here](http://www.grayslakechiropractors.com/libertyville-chiropractor-2/)

### Ricky — 2013-11-09T18:32:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycling.7334/post-100273>

Cycling is my favorite activity. Its an exercise that has many benefits. Cycling is best cardio exercise that provides us fitness. It makes legs,bones and muscles strong.

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-12T13:13:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.7336/post-87992>

Got a copy of my labs yesterday.

My TSH is 1.55 mIU/l, Free T4 17.0 pmol/L, Total T3 0.51 nmol/L (low-range is 1.3-3.1), and cortisol was 694.9 morning fasting.

Doc wasn't concerned about the Total T3 being low. Then again he barely knew what Leptin was, and could offer no advice for my fatigue, hunger, low libido, etc except 'are you eating enough fiber?' This is the doctor who has had me try every SSRI, SSNRI, TAD, and stimulant on the market for my chronic fatigue and lack of motivation.

I had asked for a leptin test but he said that he doesnt know where that can be done in Ireland. I got him to refer me to an endocrinologist in the best private hospital in Dublin, in hopes that his guy will perhaps have a clue about leptin resistance, adrenal fatigue, or whatever is going on with me.

Any insight? I'm fatigued, gaining wieght, and hungry. Would appreciate some advice.

\---------------

I am not a doctor.

Water

grounding
EMF
etc

that you have already applied (very important) advice from Jack,

### Rylee — 2013-06-13T17:46:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.7336/post-88036>

> [LRichard12 said:](/goto/post?id=87992)
>
> Got a copy of my labs yesterday.
>
> My TSH is 1.55 mIU/l, Free T4 17.0 pmol/L, Total T3 0.51 nmol/L (low-range is 1.3-3.1), and cortisol was 694.9 morning fasting.
>
> Doc wasn't concerned about the Total T3 being low. Then again he barely knew what Leptin was, and could offer no advice for my fatigue, hunger, low libido, etc except 'are you eating enough fiber?' This is the doctor who has had me try every SSRI, SSNRI, TAD, and stimulant on the market for my chronic fatigue and lack of motivation.
>
> I had asked for a leptin test but he said that he doesnt know where that can be done in Ireland. I got him to refer me to an endocrinologist in the best private hospital in Dublin, in hopes that his guy will perhaps have a clue about leptin resistance, adrenal fatigue, or whatever is going on with me.
>
> Any insight? I'm fatigued, gaining wieght, and hungry. Would appreciate some advice.

Your T3 appears to be very low, and you don't include reference ranges for your other labs so it's difficult to comment on them.

You have symptoms and (possibly) labs consistent with hypothyroidism. If you're on Facebook, find Stop The Thyroid Madness on there and join the group. I'm learning a lot from those folks - you can post your labs (with reference ranges) and others who know more about this than you or me will comment and interpret for you. Not as good as a doc, perhaps, but in some ways better. Good luck.

### gsteppe14@gmail.com — 2013-08-21T08:08:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.7336/post-93327>

You have T3, not FT3, but when your T3 is that low, I believe you can infer that your FT3 would also be low. Your endo should be concerned. Limited T4 to T3 transfer and hence you have hypothyroid symptoms, despite your TSH. If you are obese or have inflammation, your low T3 may not be reflected in your pituitary's TSH. Your tissue T3 is low; your pituitary T3 isn't. So your TSH seems normal but that doesn't mean you're not hypothyroid. So in this case, you really have to go by FT3. Get your FT3 and RevT3 measured. But it's obvious that FT3 will be low and RT3 will be high.

I'd go get an endo that uses compound pharmacies and have him prescribe custom T3 doses. The reason why you feel lousy is because you're hypothyroid. Also get those Ft4 pmol converted so we can interpret it correctly.

Your endo is not gonna know about leptin resistance. I just realized you're overseas. I really don't know to what extent compound pharmacies are available overseas. I know there're some. Go to LDN sites and see if you can find compound pharmas that prescribe LDN; they would also take doc orders for T3, not just packaged stuff like Cytomel or Armor.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-21T16:03:32+0300

[http://180degreehealth.com/2013/08/thyroid-hormones-heart-disease](http://180degreehealth.com/2013/08/thyroid-hormones-heart-disease)

# Thread: Am I in ketosis and don't even realize it?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-in-ketosis-and-dont-even-realize-it.7337/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-06-12T16:38:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-in-ketosis-and-dont-even-realize-it.7337/post-87999>

Been doing the Leptin Rx for a little over a week now.

Less than 50g of carbs every day except maybe 2 days where I was around 70g.

I can't really say that anything has felt any different and my clothes don't fit any different.

Could to much protein be keeping me out of ketosis?

Most of my BAB's are around 100g of Protein and I started eating even more fat - easily over 300 grams between foods and oils (coconut oil, olive oil and butter)

I probably consume around 180g of protein each day and could easily do more.

As I've said in many of my posts my sleep definitely isn't optimal (wake up numerous times in the night) so maybe the poor skeep is keeping me from feeling the benefits.

I've read from other people talking about ketosis that it feels awesome - more energy, feel like they are burning more fat.

I should also mention that I am only drinking fluoride free water - even got some fluoride free toothpaste as many have suggested that the fluoride could be dehydrating me and robbing me of energy.

Would be interested in hearing any thoughts.

### LinD — 2013-06-12T17:27:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-in-ketosis-and-dont-even-realize-it.7337/post-88001>

Too much protein resulting in too much glucose might be an issue.

### fireforlunch — 2013-06-12T18:24:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-in-ketosis-and-dont-even-realize-it.7337/post-88002>

Thats what i am wondering.

Hoping Doc chims in with an opinion

> [LinD said:](/goto/post?id=88001)
>
> Too much protein resulting in too much glucose might be an issue.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-12T18:47:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-in-ketosis-and-dont-even-realize-it.7337/post-88003>

what do you keto dip stix say?

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-12T21:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-in-ketosis-and-dont-even-realize-it.7337/post-88007>

For the time being I would recommend lowering your protein substantially. Eat high fat, very low - low carb, moderate protein. I would say limit protein to 150g max, preferably less than that even. If you're fat adapted you don't have to worry about losing muscle, you're body will just eat up its own body fat (even if you are extremely lean) and use dietary fat differently. Protein will be used for muscle synthesis and tissue growth/repair as well as other processes. This is where it gets fuzzy for me, though, as protein has the potential to knock you out of ketosis. Amino Acids are 48-58% percent gluconeogenic I believe - some can be used to make ketones even, some glucose, and some can be used for either. So it gets quite confusing. Different Amino's stimulate different amounts of insulin as well. Take a look at this: [http://www.lucastafur.com/search/label/high protein](http://www.lucastafur.com/search/label/high%20protein)

"So what happens when we eat a high fat/high protein diet? Let's look at Eskimos (1). On average, they consume approximately **280g of protein**, 125g of fat and 54g of carbohydrate. This gives us a total of 2461 kcal of which 45.5% is protein, 45.71% is fat, and around 8.77% is carbohydrate. This is both high protein and high fat, and very low carbohydrate. Kind of my ideal diet. When researchers studied their metabolisms, **they found that they are not in ketosis** during their usual diet. Ketosis is developed during fasting, but to a much lesser degree than other human subjects. The authors concluded:
"**Eskimos show a remarkable power to oxidize fats completely**, as evidenced by the small amount of acetone bodies excreted in the urine in fasting."

This is extremely interesting to me. I mean, it is literally fascinating to me for a variety of reasons. I really have to continue learning genetics and biochemistry to connect all the dots more in depth, but this leads me to some ideas. As you can see, regardless of the high protein count (actually, extremely high), they are still extremely fat adapted, and use fat for their biological needs. They show ketone build up during fasting, but less than typical individuals - this is probably because they utilize their body fat and dietary fat much more efficiently. The other thing is they measured acetone buildup in the urine, rather than blood/breath samples. This means that they could have actually been circulating a LOT more ketones, but they were utilizing them so efficiently, a small amount if any showed up in the urine. There are other factors that can affect the urine analysis as well. What I'm getting at is, despite the extremely high protein intake, they were still utilizing fatty acids profoundly well, and they were quite possibly in a state of deep ketosis - it just wasn't displayed due to inaccurate measurements.

Now, the reason I recommended limiting protein, is because I'm not quite sure how they (the eskimo's) could consume so much protein without (1) harmful effects (2) without GNG destroying the state of ketosis. [http://www.ketotic.org/2012/08/if-you-eat-excess-protein-does-it-turn.html](http://www.ketotic.org/2012/08/if-you-eat-excess-protein-does-it-turn.html) this is also a good read. According to Peter Attia, who is one of the only people publicly doing ketogenic experiments mixed with extreme athletic abilities, he had trouble producing ketones with up to 50g carbs and 150g protein, despite activity levels and source of carbs/protein (nuts which are slowly digesting). Other VLC advocates also advocate slightly low protein levels. But these other links display that it is quite possible to maintain ketosis and beta-oxidation while consuming lots of protein. Maybe it just takes time. So maybe you start VLC/High Fat/Moderate-moderately low protein, and after a long period (24-36mo's) you can dial up the protein and not worry about GNG from amino acids taking place.

Sorry if that was confusing or just a giant rambling, I'm just interested in this a lot. I'm actually about to go make a post comparing some ideas about ketosis/ketogenesis because as I can see from some recent readings, there are far different ideas for how one should eat a ketogenic diet and it gets even more complex in terms of physical performance.

### fireforlunch — 2013-06-13T01:49:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-in-ketosis-and-dont-even-realize-it.7337/post-88012>

Big Papa, this is awesome, thank you for the input.

I have been eating so much protein to help with hunger but usually I'm still hungry, but I'll do it.

If backing the protein puts me in ketosis and I really experience all the benefits that people talk about then a little hunger will be worth it.

I already went way over 150g today so I will start tomorrow.

I will probably skip lunch so I can have a bigger dinner.

That Peter Attia guy sure has a ton of great info.

Thank you again - absolutely fantastic

> [BigPapaChakra777 said:](/goto/post?id=88007)
>
> For the time being I would recommend lowering your protein substantially. Eat high fat, very low - low carb, moderate protein. I would say limit protein to 150g max, preferably less than that even. As you can see, regardless of the high protein count (actually, extremely high), they are still extremely fat adapted, and use fat for their biological needs. They show ketone build up during fasting, but less than typical individuals - this is probably because they utilize their body fat and dietary fat much more efficiently. The other thing is they measured acetone buildup in the urine, rather than blood/breath samples. This means that they could have actually been circulating a LOT more ketones, but they were utilizing them so efficiently, a small amount if any showed up in the urine. There are other factors that can affect the urine analysis as well. What I'm getting at is, despite the extremely high protein intake, they were still utilizing fatty acids profoundly well, and they were quite possibly in a state of deep ketosis - it just wasn't displayed due to inaccurate measurements.
>
> Now, the reason I recommended limiting protein, is because I'm not quite sure how they (the eskimo's) could consume so much protein without (1) harmful effects (2) without GNG destroying the state of ketosis. [http://www.ketotic.org/2012/08/if-you-eat-excess-protein-does-it-turn.html](http://www.ketotic.org/2012/08/if-you-eat-excess-protein-does-it-turn.html) this is also a good read. According to Peter Attia, who is one of the only people publicly doing ketogenic experiments mixed with extreme athletic abilities, he had trouble producing ketones with up to 50g carbs and 150g protein, despite activity levels and source of carbs/protein (nuts which are slowly digesting). Other VLC advocates also advocate slightly low protein levels. But these other links display that it is quite possible to maintain ketosis and beta-oxidation while consuming lots of protein. Maybe it just takes time. So maybe you start VLC/High Fat/Moderate-moderately low protein, and after a long period (24-36mo's) you can dial up the protein and not worry about GNG from amino acids taking place.
>
> Sorry if that was confusing or just a giant rambling, I'm just interested in this a lot. I'm actually about to go make a post comparing some ideas about ketosis/ketogenesis because as I can see from some recent readings, there are far different ideas for how one should eat a ketogenic diet and it gets even more complex in terms of physical performance.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-13T02:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-in-ketosis-and-dont-even-realize-it.7337/post-88014>

No problem, glad I can help lead you in the direction of some more information. That's what's great about forums like these, we can all partake in each others journey's to recovery/well-being/enhanced performance/etc. Peter Attia's blog is definitely one of my favorites. I had created a word document composed of hundreds of blog posts, video presentations, and scholarly articles on ketosis, I haven't gotten to reading it yet as my fiance has my laptop and I have hers lol. I'm going to create a post soon (maybe tonight or tomorrow night) on the differences in ketogenic diets because I have noticed a lot, especially if you look at the experiments of Peter Attia vs. that of Ben Greenfield - Peter Attia is extremely active and still limits carbs to below 50, whereas Ben Greenfield consumes 50-100g of carbs on an "easy day" in his current ketogenic experiment. There are differences in beliefs of protein, and even the kinds of fats that should be consumed (polyunsaturated vs. saturated vs. mct). So many variables to target. I'm hoping some more knowledgeable people, or even Dr. Kruse will comment when I post it because I feel as though it will clarify things not only for me, but for many others.

# Thread: Reversing damage caused by adderall...

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread

### MonteD — 2013-06-13T08:24:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reversing-damage-caused-by-adderall.7341/post-88019>

I have a friend who has been prescribed adderall 20mg for 8 years now. She is 28. She's really desperate to quit the drug but feels like she is a zombie without it. I don't think it's a case of addiction either because she can quit for months at a time. Each time though its like the pleasure and motivation parts of the brain just aren't there.

Reading up on it I see that the **nucleus accumbens** of the brain are damaged by amphetamine use, the portion of the brain responsible for motivation and drive. In other words adderall eventually turns people into listless underachievers,especially when they come off of it.

Any one here have any ideas on a strategy to repair this part of the brain? I know one of Jack's biggest points is brain plasticity.

Jack? Anyone?

### Grizz — 2013-06-13T16:00:00+0300

Google **_adderall lawsuits_**

Grizz

### Trey625 — 2013-06-15T14:22:12+0300

Its only been recently that things are starting to get better after 9 months of Dr K's protocols..i just hope i can heal all the damage that i feel was done.. Cant wait for that webinar!!! I feel for all the kids on that drug...how do they ever come off it without turning to other drugs when they have spent their whole childhood on it??? Guess thats one of the reasons Meth is gaining in popularity among teens and lives are being destroyed..its so sad

### kathylu — 2013-06-14T04:23:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-you-seen-this-video-about-smart-meters.7345/post-88069>

I'm more concerned about the EMF than I am about the political implications.

### Dextery — 2013-06-14T04:43:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-you-seen-this-video-about-smart-meters.7345/post-88071>

> [kathylu said:](/goto/post?id=88069)
>
> I'm more concerned about the EMF than I am about the political implications.

You can shield your meter by the following cheap fix:

For $10, everyone can shield their smart meter. Get a one gallon paint can, clean it up or buy an unused one at home depot. Also buy a small role of aluminum screening and also buy two 7 inch hose clamps. Cut the bottom out of the paint can and place it over the meter. Use one of the hose clamps to secure it to the smart meter. The can is a little larger than the meter. The can will bulge a bit when secured tightly. Then cut a piece of the aluminum screening that will fit over the end of the can facing away from the wall it is mounted on. Use the other hose clamp to secure the screen material. Trim as necessary. Take the remaining screening material and mount it behind the meter on the back side of the wall the meter is attached to. This prevents the emf from the meter radiating back through the wall as the meter is omnidirectional. The meter can still transmit your electrical usage to the power company. For people with the meter mounted on a pole or elsewhare, get the aluminum screening and drape the screen over the meter and all around the back side of the pole.
You are now shielded 95%. I don't have an emf meter, but the youtube videos I have seen do things similar to this and they block the radiation quite well.

Edit: You also need to ground the paint can with a wire. I just slip one bare end under one of the hose clamps and then run it down to a piece of copper wire I bought from Home Depot...about 3 ft...buried it with the grounding wire attached.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-14T15:34:18+0300

**Posts:** 5

### Luella — 2013-06-14T11:27:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-scotland.7348/post-88075>

As the daughter of a postman, our household lights were out at 9pm and we were all asleep by 10pm at the latest - guess what? We were all skinny! Now I am making the connection about light, leptin and sleep, it's my goal to get back to where I used to be on the scales when I lived that lifestyle. I am also trying to implement some of the stuff I have learned on this forum with my own kids. Excited to be on this journey. Although bearing in mind that how light it is in Scotland at 11pm at this time of the year, it's almost impossible to create total darkness in the bedroom!

### Inger — 2013-06-28T08:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-scotland.7348/post-89121>

Welcome Luella! It isn't dark here either in the evenings, I live in Finland. But guess what! I do not even care anymore. I need to have the window wide open to get fresh air in, so it really is not dark at all in my bedroom when I go to sleep. I still get so tired at 10 PM! I have no issues fall into sleep at all. Weird huh! Maybe we are adapted to sleep in not so dark environment in summer.  Or all the darkness in winter makes us tolerate some light in the night summertime? I do has issues with sleeping in the summer until I started the Kruse protocols though, so I bet if we just keep it dark and cold in the winter it wont matter if bedroom is not dark at all right now.

I also take care to get lots and lots sun at daytime, and run outside barefoot scantily clad all the time. Maybe that helps too? I always go by natural. I trust my body will be be able to go by the seasons. When I just let it feel them.

My wife has spent the last 7 days in the Progressive Care Unit of our local hospital with severe pneumonia in both lungs. She is immuno-suppressed due to years of taking rheumatoid drugs. Her improvement is slight, and the outcome is still in doubt. She has been placed on a liquid diet but has a huge hunger...I have been making her protein shakes and taking them into the hospital.

I am having a hard time thinking straight. I will gladly accept any advice on how I can help her.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-request-for-support-and-advice.7349/post-88078>

What about adding some fat to her shakes? I would added slow. The added fat should help with her hunger.

### Destiny — 2013-06-14T13:47:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-request-for-support-and-advice.7349/post-88079>

I was taking it with zinc when my WBC went low. I am not sure how much it brought it up since I am going for a blood test in a couple of days.

Here is the link of the one I used.

[http://www.amazon.com/Optimal-Lipos...371206679&sr=8-3&keywords=liposomal+vitamin+c](http://www.amazon.com/Optimal-Liposomal-Vitamin-Non-Soy-Servings/dp/B007WUR5AO/ref=sr_1_3?ie=UTF8&qid=1371206679&sr=8-3&keywords=liposomal+vitamin+c)

### NittDan — 2013-07-01T23:25:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-request-for-support-and-advice.7349/post-89317>

We have been to hell and back since my last update... DW ended up on a ventilator two days later. She was desaturating very quickly every time they tried to get her out of bed. Saturday they did a tracheostomy. She was not responding to any treatment...antibacterial, antiviral, and steroids. The assumption became that her rheumatoid was the primary problem. CT scan performed yesterday contradicited the rheumatoid theory. She has responded well since the trach, and may be done with the ventilator. Now breathing on her own through trach tube with the help of some oxygen.

Thanks so much for your thoughts, prayers and advice.

# Thread: Saccharomyces on a GI Fx test anyone?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saccharomyces-on-a-gi-fx-test-anyone.7353/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2013-06-14T15:35:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saccharomyces-on-a-gi-fx-test-anyone.7353/post-88092>

Hi all,

have any of you ever had Saccharomyces sp. show up on your GI Fx test while you are also supplementing with S. boulardii??

And if so, did your MD tell you to treat that yeast.....? Mine did but it doesn't make sense to me.

Why'd I want to kill or root out something I'm also populating with?

### melrito — 2013-06-15T22:18:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saccharomyces-on-a-gi-fx-test-anyone.7353/post-88127>

**s. boulardii**

> [Hope said:](/goto/post?id=88092)
>
> Hi all,
>
> have any of you ever had Saccharomyces sp. show up on your GI Fx test while you are also supplementing with S. boulardii??
>
> And if so, did your MD tell you to treat that yeast.....? Mine did but it doesn't make sense to me.
>
> Why'd I want to kill or root out something I'm also populating with?

Finding s. boulardii on a Gi Fx test doesn't mean you have to treat yeast.... since s. boulardii is considered a beneficial yeast (hence the supplementation with it). Bad yeast such as candida albicans is generally considered an opportunistic pathogen - meaning that until you fix the environment (fixyour gut), the yeast will continue to proliferate. Supplementing with s. boulardii is actually beneficial against bad yeast, as it can inhibit the reproduction of yeast - at least that is the case with candida albicans. If you choose to treat yeast (if you have a yeast overgrowth), just be aware that you will need to do some major gut flora rebuilding afterward.

### Hope — 2013-07-12T17:24:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saccharomyces-on-a-gi-fx-test-anyone.7353/post-89955>

yeah...thanks Melrito!

# Thread: Help me understand this study? omega-3 fatty acids induce leptin resistance?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-understand-this-study-omega-3-fatty-acids-induce-leptin-resistance.7357/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Albert83BCN — 2013-06-15T16:32:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-understand-this-study-omega-3-fatty-acids-induce-leptin-resistance.7357/post-88116>

Today I stumbled into this study and got my head scratching. Seems to be the very opposite of what the Epi-Paleo protocol says: omega-3 fatty acids induce leptin resistance? Correlation between weight gain and omega-3 administration? Should't be the opposite? Don't they help lower inflammation at the hypothalamus and account for better energy status via leptin?

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413105001129](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413105001129)

I know that mice are not the same as humans, but seems really weird. I am missing something? Aren't the mice cold adapted?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-15T19:56:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-understand-this-study-omega-3-fatty-acids-induce-leptin-resistance.7357/post-88118>

Many reasons why this study is bad. DHA without iodine is not the same as epi paleo.......why? DHA is a PUFA and very subject to oxidation as I have pointed out many times on the blog.......Fish oil will never equal real food. I suggest you re read Brain Gut 5 many times.

Mice never equal humans. We can cure cancer in mice and rats but the same effect is never found in humans........why? From Dr. Tufur

Using animal studies to propose novel hypotheses can be fun. However, we cannot extrapolate findings directly. This is important when discussing metabolic pathways and the effect of different diets. For instance, mice have a basal metabolic rate that is 7 times greater than humans, so a 40% calorie restriction in mice mimics a therapeutic fasting in humans. There is evidence that mRNA levels of several lipogenic enzymes are different between rats and humans. In general, the lipogenic capacity of adipose tissue is lower in humans than rats, which may be explained by decreased ChREBP mRNA expression and lower abundance of SREBP1c . But it is interesting to note that expression of ChREBP differs in adipose tissue and liver. In obese subjects, hepatic ChREBP expression increases while decreasing in adipose tissue, and there is a significant increase of ChREBP mRNA and protein levels during preadipocyte differentiation.

1\. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16709251/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16709251/)
2.[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12897191?dopt=Abstract](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12897191?dopt=Abstract)
3.[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21840420](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21840420)

When you know better you do better.......knowing about mice and rats is good for mice and rats and researchers trying to formulate and test hypothesis..........they try to generalize their findings in mice and rats to humans to get grant money from governments........it is the business of how science gets done.......and it creates this perception you have..........

Never rely on these studies........study things carefully and then look at your species. Your species is where the gold lies. Brain gut 1-5 tell you how humans came to be. re read it. Read the cites........the information is overwhelming that mice and rats never equal us.......in fact primate data does not even generalize.......I have told Rosedale many times that Calorie restriction in primates wont match humans.......I said it in CT 4 and Ct6. And the early data says I am right to believe it. He still does not get why..........mTOR and IGF1 pathways are only bad when inflammation is present.......when it is not they both help longevity in humans.

### Albert83BCN — 2013-06-15T20:24:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-understand-this-study-omega-3-fatty-acids-induce-leptin-resistance.7357/post-88121>

# Thread: For those questioning mainstream science
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/for-those-questioning-mainstream-science.7362/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SeaHorse — 2013-06-16T13:35:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/for-those-questioning-mainstream-science.7362/post-88156>

I've been getting updates from the Thunderbolt project and this meeting was announced recently. It is good to realize that many of the sciences have their outliers and questioners. Gerald Pollack (the water guy) will be at this meeting too.

ON THE NATURAL PHILOSOPHY ALLIANCE AND ITS ANNUAL MEETING:

World-Wide Science Think Tank Returns to the University of Maryland July 2013 to Address Topics Outside the Mainstream

College Park, Maryland (May 15, 2013) - The Natural Philosophy Alliance (NPA), a world-wide think tank, is holding its 20th Annual Conference at the University of Maryland, College Park, from July 10 -13, 2013, in conjunction with the Conference on Future Energy (COFE). This yearâ€™s conference will feature over 70 authors on various scientific topics including presentations on Relativity, Structures, Gravity, the Big Bang, Expansion Tectonics, Electric Universe, and Science Philosophy. The NPA will also be presenting their annual Sagnac Award, given to the best work in science outside the mainstream. This is the third year in which the NPA networked with other conferences, having held earlier events with the Electric Universe group and the annual Tesla Tech conference.

This yearâ€™s NPA conference will feature prominent members Dr. Bill Lucas, Dr. Cynthia Whitney, Dr. Gerald Pollack, and Wal Thornhill. Dr. Lucas, Dr. Whitney, and Dr. Pollack will be talking about their ground-breaking books including â€œThe Universal Force" (Volume 1) by Dr. Lucas, "Algebraic Chemistry: Applications and Origins" by Dr. Cynthia Whitney, and â€œThe Fourth Phase of Waterâ€￾ by Dr. Gerald Pollack.

Dr. Whitney will deliver the annual John Chappell Lecture in honor of NPA founder Dr. John Chappell. Stephen Smith, managing editor of the popular Thunderbolts PIcture of the Day, will give a presentation on â€œMars: The Great Desertâ€￾ in 3-D, and David Talbott, director of the Thunderbolts Project, will speak on "Exposing the Myths of Settled Science."

This yearâ€™s Sagnac Award will be going to Wal Thornhill, a pioneer of the Electric Universe paradigm. NPA members consider the Sagnac Award to be the "Nobel Prize" for scientists working outside the mainstream.

On Thursday, there will be an outside demonstration by Dr. Max Fomitchev-Zamilov of cavitation-induced fusion. Also, on Saturday afternoon, there will be a panel discussion on the NPA and the future of science.

About the Natural Philosophy Alliance (NPA) - The Natural Philosophy Alliance (NPA) is a world-wide scientific organization whose members have one thing in common: they have intensely studied mainstream theories in the sciences, including relativity, the big bang, and plate tectonics. Many members have taken a critical position on standard theoretical assumptions. The NPA's goal is to seek truth in science through observation, experiment, and critical reasoning. The organization invites members to vigorously evaluate competing theories, enabling the most compelling ideas to grow and flourish through the unfettered pursuit of scientific knowledge. See more at [http://www.worldnpa.org](http://www.worldnpa.org).

About the Sagnac Award - The Natural Philosophy Alliance (NPA), a world-wide think-tank and scientific organization chooses one or more scientists each year to receive a Lifetime Achievement Award for their groundbreaking work outside the mainstream of science. The Award is named after Georges Sagnac, a French physicist who lent his name to the Sagnac effect, a discovery that made possible both interferometers and ring laser gyroscopes developed since the 1970s.

About the Thunderbolts Project - An interdisciplinary collaboration of accredited scientists and independent researchers established in 2004. Its prime mission is to explore the Electric Universe paradigm. Recent discoveries in the sciences have placed a spotlight on the electromagnetic force in nature, from quantum worlds and biological systems to planetary, stellar, and galactic domains. More information at [http://www.thunderbolts.info](http://www.thunderbolts.info)

Natural Philosophy Alliance
Tel: 310-991-5744
[david@worldnpa.org](mailto:david@worldnpa.org)
Mikamar Publishing

16871 SE 80th Pl
Milwaukie, OR
97267
US
